top_tweets.author: shcallaway
top_tweets.text: This simple chart captures one of my biggest learnings from the past 9 months of building AI agents at @11x_official. To explain: adding structure to your agent can provide performance gains in the short-term, but this structure can actually make your agent perform _worse_ in the long-term (as the underlying models get better). Credit to @swyx for surfacing the chart in his latest blog post, "In the Matter of OpenAI vs LangGraph", and for re-framing the chart in terms of agent performance (as opposed to model performance). Credit to @hwchung27 for the original!

top_tweets.text: Creative Your Burnout Morning Poster (Customized to Your Personality & Aesthetic) Created using @AdobeFirefly Partnered Model Prompt - Use my uploaded face as the ONLY facial reference. Preserve my exact identity, facial structure, expressive eyes, skin tone, lips, nose shape, and recognizable features with zero facial drift. Create an ultra-detailed cinematic 3D chibi-style portrait inspired by premium Pixar and modern collectible character art. The character should represent an exhausted but genius-level AI creator girl after an all-night creative session. Scene: She is sitting sleepily on the edge of a rustic wooden table early in the morning, looking emotionally drained but unintentionally adorable. Messy fluffy hair tied loosely in a chaotic bun with strands falling everywhere, sleepy half-open eyes, soft pout, slightly red nose, tired “I edited all night” expression. She wears oversized pastel sleepwear with subtle creative-tech aesthetics: soft ivory pajama set tiny “Future Vibes AI” embroidery cozy cardigan slipping off one shoulder fluffy duck slippers delicate pearl earrings Environment: Warm moody morning atmosphere with soft cinematic lighting, steam rising from a cup of coffee beside her, blurred artistic studio background, creative workspace energy. Around the character include subtle hints of her personality: tiny floating holographic video timelines miniature film clapperboard AI art sketches tiny glowing travel icons scattered sticky notes with creative ideas small laptop showing unfinished edits scribbled concepts YouTube play icon soft pastel doodles Expression & Mood: She should look like: an overthinking creative genius emotionally exhausted but lovable sleep-deprived filmmaker energy “just one more edit” vibe quiet chaos mixed with soft humor Style: Ultra-premium stylized realism, collectible figurine quality, ultra-detailed hair strands, realistic fabric texture, soft skin shading, cinematic depth of field, dreamy muted pastel tones, cozy Pinterest aesthetic, subtle humor, emotional storytelling. Lighting: Soft window light, warm cinematic shadows, gentle volumetric haze, realistic steam glow from coffee. Color Palette: Mocha brown, warm cream, dusty peach, muted teal, ivory, soft caramel, pastel gold accents. Composition: Centered symmetrical composition, shallow depth of field, highly expressive face, clean luxury aesthetic, social-media-ready, 8K ultra detailed. Add tiny handwritten doodles around the scene: “rendering…” “creative chaos” “future vibes” “1% battery” “one more export” Negative Prompt: bad anatomy, distorted face, extra fingers, plastic skin, overexposed highlights, blurry eyes, cartoonish proportions, low detail, bad hands, uncanny face, oversaturated colors, duplicate objects, warped furniture, messy composition, low resolution.

top_tweets.author: timsoulo
top_tweets.text: In the last 6 months at @Ahrefs, we analyzed over 1 billion data points across 14 studies. Here's what we learned about AI search optimization: 1) "Best X" blog listicles are the single most prominent content format cited by AI chatbots. They make up 43.8% of all page types cited by ChatGPT specifically. 2) 67% of ChatGPT's top 1,000 citations come from sources marketers can't influence: Wikipedia (29.7%), homepages (23.8%), app stores (6.6%). Only 32.3% are influenceable content like educational pages, reviews, news, and blog posts. 3) 28.3% of ChatGPT's most-cited pages have zero Google organic visibility. These pages get cited repeatedly by ChatGPT despite not ranking in Google at all. A completely separate discovery layer. 4) ChatGPT only cites about 50% of the URLs it retrieves. It fetches dozens of pages per query but uses half as background context without attribution. This means that being retrieved and being cited are very different things. 5) Adding schema markup had zero meaningful impact on AI citations. AI Overviews actually dipped −4.6%, while AI Mode (+2.4%) and ChatGPT (+2.2%) showed changes indistinguishable from zero. 6) YouTube mentions have the highest correlation (0.737) with AI brand visibility out of all the factors we studied (including all the conventional SEO metrics like backlinks, page count, DR, etc). This held true for both Google-owned and OpenAI products. 7) AI Overviews reduce clicks to the #1 result by 58%. That’s up from 34.5% just 10 months earlier. The trend is accelerating. 8) 99.9% of AI Overviews appear on informational intent queries. Transactional, navigational, and local searches are almost entirely AIO-free. Shopping triggers AIOs just 3.2% of the time. 9) For a given search query, Google’s AI Mode and AI Overviews reach the same conclusions 86% of the time — but cite almost entirely different sources (only 13.7% citation overlap). 10) AI Overviews change every 2.15 days on average, with 70% of content differing between consecutive observations. But semantic similarity stays at 0.95. The words, sources, and entities constantly shuffle, but the actual meaning barely moves.

top_tweets.author: timsoulo
top_tweets.text: Once you build your first AI app, you're done for. Every single process in your day starts looking like something you should automate. Glen Allsopp [ @ViperChill ] (Head of Marketing Strategy & Research at @Ahrefs) showed me a perfect example of this. He used Agent A for competitive backlink research. Got exactly what he needed. Then typed: "please turn this into an application." Three minutes later he had a reusable tool that analyzes backlinks across multiple websites in 20 seconds. Cost per run: less than one cent. Then he did the same with his task management workflow. Then with content research. Then with marketing copy. Then with AI visibility reports. Now almost every part of his workflow has a custom tool built around it So I invited Glen on Ahrefs Podcast to demo all of these tools live on screen, walk through how he built them with AI and what he learned in the process. Here's what we covered: 🔸 Why only 10% of marketers have tried building anything with AI 🔸 The "imagination gap" that stops most people from even starting 🔸 How a conversation with AI becomes a reusable marketing tool in 3 minutes 🔸 Why the cheapest AI model is often the right one for marketing tasks 🔸 Why your personal task manager should be built by you, not bought 🔸 Why copying someone's AI app is just the starting point, not a finished product 🔸 Why having marketing expertise matters more now, not less Watch/Listen here 👇 https://t.co/mQeJsERIdY P.S. If you enjoy these deep dives, a quick "Like" or comment helps us convince more great guests to come on the show. Thanks! 🙏

top_tweets.author: getbluejay_ai
top_tweets.text: Thank you so much to everyone who came out to the Low Latency Club meetup at @telnyx last night and shared what they're building in voice AI! Big thanks to Telnyx and @AssemblyAI for hosting and co-sponsoring, and a huge thank you to all the speakers and attendees who made the night so valuable. A big shoutout to our CEO @RohanVasishth , Anusha Thruval, and @ryanseams for sharing their insights throughout the night. A few themes that came up throughout the night: • Real-world audio conditions (background noise, AirPods on a busy street) are where speech models are truly tested, and closing that gap is still one of the hardest problems in the space. • Latency is a full-stack challenge. Carrier hops, inference speed, and context window size all add up, and owning your infrastructure makes a real difference. • Inconsistent quality in voice agents usually comes down to orchestration and configuration, not just the model itself. • Giving voice agents dynamic context in real time remains one of the biggest unsolved problems, especially in high-stakes conversations. See you at the next one. 👋

top_tweets.text: 2026 marks a decade since I moved to the U.S. for school. After 10 years of learning, navigating academia, and finding my communities along the way, I am SO excited to share that I've received my Ph.D. from @cmuhcii, @CarnegieMellon and joined @MSFTResearch (MSR), New York City as a Senior Researcher!!! At MSR, I am thrilled to be able to work at the intersection of research, product, and policy within the 𝐅𝐀𝐓𝐄 (𝐅𝐚𝐢𝐫𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬, 𝐀𝐜𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲, 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐲, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐄𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐀𝐈) 𝐠𝐫𝐨𝐮𝐩 and closely with the 𝐒𝐓𝐀𝐂 (𝐒𝐨𝐜𝐢𝐨-𝐓𝐞𝐜𝐡𝐧𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐀𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐂𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫) 𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐦 — partnering with product teams across Microsoft to translate rigorous research into practice. My goal for the next stage of my career is to 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐳𝐞 𝐀𝐈 𝐞𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐦𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡 𝐢𝐧 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥-𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐥𝐝 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐩𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 to ensure safer and more responsible AI, with a particular focus on building tools and processes to evaluate agentic AI systems and supporting meaningful human engagement in human–agent collaboration. I am tremendously grateful to everyone who's been part of this journey, and excited for what's ahead!!

top_tweets.author: rsalakhu
top_tweets.text: Congrats to the Webwright team https://t.co/mmpl4tO0p4 at @MSFTResearch for taking the #1 spot on Odysseys, a highly challenging benchmark for long-horizon web agents: https://t.co/rj5BHK5g6C Odysseys evaluates realistic, multi-hour web workflows that require sustained planning, memory, reasoning, and verification across many websites and tools. These are far beyond short single-step browser tasks. For example, if you are searching for CS faculty positions, a single task could involve building a comprehensive Excel tracker of openings across the top CS schools using CSRankings as the master checklist; verifying every school directly through department, engineering, and university careers pages for CS/AI/ML/data science/robotics/vision faculty roles; opening and validating each posting; maintaining structured evidence and verification tabs; and finishing with a completeness audit and summary of hiring trends. Exciting progress toward truly capable long-horizon web agents.

top_tweets.author: MaxMusing
top_tweets.text: We came to this exact conclusion at @Basedash which led us to what we're building today. Basedash: 1. Connects to your systems of record (Salesforce, Slack, Notion, GitHub, Linear, etc.) 2. Builds a context graph across all those systems 3. Finds insights and makes decisions for you That being said, I disagree that warehouses are the wrong data layer. Agree that current players are not well positioned, but MCPs (and equivalent) are fundamentally flawed for the read path. SQL is so much more expressive and capable than the patchwork of APIs and MCP servers for each system of record. The quality of insights and decisions we can get out of Basedash is 10x better than anything I've seen from other agents (and most humans 😄). We haven't even touched the write path yet though. In my opinion this is the (relatively) easy part 🤞

top_tweets.author: MarioNawfal
top_tweets.text: When was the last time you called your customer and asked him - Hey, can I do something for you? We know that's a hard task if your business has thousands of customers. But someone has figured out an elegant way. @usebland is the first tool that allows anyone to build Production-Ready Voice Agents from a single Prompt. What the hell does that mean? Norm, which is a part of the Bland interface, lets you automate thousands, even millions of phone calls with a single prompt. Bland is fully self-hosted, meaning your data stays entirely secure from end-to-end. And it has the lowest latency on the planet, making your calls sound human. Each customer gets their own dedicated instance, ensuring maximum security, reliability, and control. You can deploy your instance within the Bland infrastructure, on-premise, or in your VPC. We need to repeat this because it is insanely cool: one prompt - millions of phone calls. Know more on https://t.co/SSaCobMn7K and tune in to their live show at 1:30 pm EST. Disclaimer: This content was produced in collaboration with the client and is intended for informational purposes only. It does not constitute financial or investment advice. Always conduct your own research before making any financial decisions.

top_tweets.author: trynullsec
top_tweets.text: AI-generated software is becoming its own software supply chain. @cursor_ai, @Lovable, @Replit, @boltdotnew,@claudeai, @grok, agents, dApps, MCP tools, all of them are moving code from prompt to production faster than traditional security workflows can keep up. That creates a new requirement: security validation has to become model-native. Nullsec-S1 is being built for that layer. A security-specialized LLM that sits between AI-generated code and production, analyzing auth logic, secrets, env exposure, SQLi, SSRF, XSS, MCP/tool abuse, shell execution, dependency risk, wallet flows, and safe-code calibration. AI builds the software. Nullsec-S1 validates it.

top_tweets.author: GovernorShapiro
top_tweets.text: These AI companies are preying on our kids. We sued @character_ai to hold them accountable and keep our kids safe.

top_tweets.author: GovernorShapiro
top_tweets.text: .@character_ai has over 20 million monthly users — including kids and other vulnerable Pennsylvanians who trust the information they’re getting from these AI chatbots. When my AI Task Force discovered that @character_ai was presenting their chatbot as a licensed psychiatrist in Pennsylvania and providing users with medical advice, we took action. These bad actors are putting Pennsylvanians at risk — and we’re going to do everything we can to stop them.

top_tweets.author: PAGovOffice
top_tweets.text: NEW on @MTLegalAF: @GovernorShapiro describes how @Character_AI misrepresented a chatbot as a licensed medical professional and why his Administration is taking them to court: "This lawsuit is so important to root out this type of technology, these apps, the props for these apps that are making life harder for our kids."

top_tweets.author: PAGovOffice
top_tweets.text: LIVE ON @CNN with @jaketapper: @GovernorShapiro talks his Administration's lawsuit against @character_ai over chatbots posing as licensed medical professionals: "About 30% of teens use these types of chatbots. GPT image 2.0 on @ChatGPTapp Prompt: Create an ultra-premium luxury advertising poster for a famous snack brand, designed by a world-class graphic designer, luxury branding expert, and award-winning advertising art director. Incorporate cinematic lighting, realistic water droplets, condensation effects, glossy reflections, premium splash effects, floating particles, seasoning dust, and ultra-detailed textures that emphasize freshness, quality, and flavor. top_tweets.author: _philschmid
top_tweets.text: Read the technical reports on how @Kimi_Moonshot, @cursor_ai, and @trychroma train vertical agentic models with RL. Same underlying recipe, strong base model, train inside the production harness, outcome-based rewards. - Kimi K2.5 learns to spawn parallel sub-agents through RL. -Cursor uses the same production Harness (same tools, same prompts..) and leanrs self-summarization during RL. - Chroma's 20B retrieval model learns to prune its own context mid-search. Full write-up 👇

top_tweets.author: zmh
top_tweets.text: 🎉 Some very exciting news: @meshhq has been acquired by @automattic! Clay will live on as a standalone product and continue to grow—with our entire team remaining at the helm, more resources, and broader reach. Automattic is the parent company of @WordPress (which powers over 40% of the internet) and other fantastic products like @beeper, @tumblr, @dayone, @pocketcasts, and more. Clay has 150 million relationships under management and growing, with everyone from Fortune 500 execs to students using it daily. We’re very thankful to our talented team, amazing investors, and loyal members for being the true champions of this tremendous milestone. I also need to pause here and specially thank my co-founder @Achariam, whose product vision, design excellence, and dedication turned Clay from an idea into the product and business it is today. Building a company is hard. Building one with a co-founder and friend like Matt is a rare gift. That said, we’re not going anywhere! A few things: 1️⃣ Our product and team will be growing, unlike every previous app or product in this space. We started Clay because we had been burned by spending days adding people and notes into apps that eventually shut down. Plaxo, Contactually, Connected, Refresh, Rapportive, Accompany, Brewster, Humin, Xobni, Gist, Cobook, Soocial, RelateIQ — all abandoned or shut down. Clay is a paid product where you are the customer. And Automattic has a number of beautifully-designed, continually-maintained, privacy-conscious apps like Beeper, Day One Journal, and Pocket Casts that have the same philosophy. We are joining the pantheon of beautifully-designed, privacy-respecting, tech-advancing apps. Automattic is celebrating its 20th anniversary this month. It thinks in 100 year increments, and keeps apps running for decades. 2️⃣ We are your best choice for anything contacts, network, or CRM related. If you believe in any part of our mission, join us. Our team is obsessed with building a great product for your people and relationships, and we ship daily. If you’ve ever wanted a better way to send gifts to friends, or remember birthdays, or scan business cards, or intro people to each other, or search your LinkedIn connections with AI, we either do it today or we will soon. Sign up for free, and say hi at care@clay.earth! 3️⃣ Digital identity and relationship graphs are more important than ever. As AI continues to power our everyday software, your contacts, network, messages, people, and relationships are the last, most sensitive piece of the puzzle. That information shouldn’t be part of LLM training data, ever. But when done locally, just for you, with your permission, it can unlock magical product experiences that bring us closer together. We’re excited to have you with us on this journey, and we look forward to building a brighter, more connected world together. So — thank you for being a part of the Clay community. Let’s build something extraordinary together.

top_tweets.author: ainativedev
top_tweets.text: The NOW track at DevCon - this is the stuff that actually works today. Not demos. Not beta waitlists. Production-ready. You'll hear from practitioners who've already shipped: - @bdougieyo (Head of DX, @continuedev) on virtual tool calling and portable AI toolchains. This is about making your AI integrations work across different environments without rewriting everything. - @nnennahacks ~ AI and Emerging Technology (Principal Developer Advocate, @QodoAI ) explaining why you need separation of agentic concerns. One AI model trying to do everything is a recipe for disaster. She's seen it fail. - @renebrandel (Cofounder & CEO, @getcasco) sharing how they hacked the YC Spring 2025 batch's AI agents. Real tactics from someone who just did it. - Sneha Tuli (Principal Product Manager, @Microsoft) on AI-assisted code reviews at scale. Not the promise - the actual implementation. Quality and security when every PR has AI fingerprints on it. This isn't about what's coming. It's about what's working right now that you can use Monday morning. 📍 DevCon is November 18-19th in NYC. 🎫 Tickets and full lineup: https://t.co/UgYxB5bi4Z

top_tweets.author: Scrapelessteam
top_tweets.text: We’re proud to announce that Scrapeless is now an official sponsor of @crawl4ai , the #1 trending GitHub repo for blazing-fast, AI-ready web crawling. ⚡ https://t.co/wfAZDC69Pg This partnership accelerates our shared mission: making intelligent, large-scale web crawling faster, more reliable, and easier for developers to adopt. Scrapeless brings production-grade infrastructure for Crawling, Automation, and AI Agents, including: Scraping Browser — a cloud browser built for automated workflows and large-scale extraction: high concurrency, low-latency session isolation, and advanced stealth fingerprinting to evade modern anti-scraping defenses. Four proxy types — Residential, ISP, Datacenter, and IPv6 proxies so teams can choose the right routing and access strategy across regions and network types. Universal Scraping API — real-time block-bypassing, fast data fetches, and native handling for dynamic content and anti-bot systems. Customizable data solutions — enterprise-grade options and tailored approaches for AI chat platforms like Perplexity and ChatGPT. Together with Crawl4AI, we’re building an open, extensible, developer-friendly ecosystem for AI-driven data collection. Stay tuned — we’ll be rolling out integration examples, best-practice workflows, and ready-made templates to help you supercharge your Crawl4AI pipelines. 👉 https://t.co/RtcukHX00u #Scrapeless #Crawl4AI #Crawl4AIxScrapeless #AI #ArtificialIntelligence #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #GenAI #AIAgents #AIInfrastructure #AIDevelopment #MLOps #WebScraping #WebCrawling #DataExtraction #ScrapingBrowser #AntiBot #StealthScraping #HighConcurrency #CrawlingInfrastructure #ProxyNetwork #ResidentialProxies #ISPProxies #DatacenterProxies #IPv6Proxies #ScrapingTools #UniversalScrapingAPI #Developers #DevTools #OpenSource #GitHub #Coding #BackendEngineering #CloudComputing #Automation #AutomationTools #IntelligentAutomation #CloudAutomation #DataPipeline #DataEngineering #BigData #AIDrivenCrawling #BuildWithScrapeless #CrawlBetter #ProductUpdate #TechNews #Innovation #StartupLife #Partnership #PartnershipAnnouncement

top_tweets.author: akshay_pachaar
top_tweets.text: this is the most underrated update in the agent space right now. your AI workflow runs for 47 minutes, burns 312 LLM calls, then crashes at step 8. most frameworks make you restart from zero. @crewAIInc just shipped checkpointing. think google docs autosave, but for your agent's work-in-progress. every flow method becomes a recovery point. resume in one line. fork from any saved state into a new branch. edit past outputs and watch changes ripple downstream. visual TUI to inspect everything. your pipelines stop being fragile one-shot jobs. they become resumable, inspectable, branchable processes. zero extra infra. 100% open-source. get started with CrewAI here: https://t.co/bPGE0cGOIm (don't forget to star it ⭐️)

top_tweets.author: gracegongGG
top_tweets.text: What do the CEO of @Snowflake, the CTO of @vercel, the VP of @cursor_ai, and I have in common? We're all speaking at Snowflake Dev Day on June 4. Alongside: The founder of @FactoryAI. The Head of Developer Experience at @OpenAI. The founder of @crewAIInc. The GP of @BlitzVentures. The career ladder everyone grew up climbing doesn't really exist anymore. The job market is rough, and AI isn't just helping us work, it's starting to do the work. So we're doing something about it. My session is an open AMA: Grow Your Career in the Age of AI. Arnnon Geshuri, Snowflake's Chief People Officer, is leading our panel. Voices from venture capital, academia, and the economy, getting real about what's changing, what to learn, and where the opportunities are. Come with your real questions. This is the room I've been working toward for years. The event is FREE. Registration link in the first comment. Beyond excited to be speaking alongside icons like @RamaswmySridhar, Benoit Dageville, @DenisePersson, Arnnon Geshuri, @joaomdmoura, @romainhuet, @cramforce, @tidocarriero, @matanSF, @jowyang, and @scottbudman. See you June 4. 🚀 #SnowflakeDevDay #AI #VentureCapital #FutureOfWork #AgenticAI

top_tweets.author: ApacheSpark
top_tweets.text: For a long time, streaming architecture advice boiled down to two engines: one for high-throughput ETL, another when you need millisecond latency. At Data Engineering Open Forum 2026, Indrajit Roy (@databricks) walked through how Apache Spark Structured Streaming took a different path from day one: micro-batch processing. 🔸 Micro-batch model: Records arrive on a stream; the engine waits briefly, forms a batch, processes it, then repeats. 🔸 Batch query on stream slices: Each step is effectively running a small batch query over the latest slice of data. 🔸 One engine, different tradeoffs: The design challenges the “two streaming engines” default instead of accepting it as fixed. Watch the full keynote: https://t.co/mpQstlPVSF #ApacheSpark #StructuredStreaming #DataEngineering #OpenSource

top_tweets.author: kwindla
top_tweets.text: Congratulations to the @DeepgramAI team on this integration of seamless multi-lingual support into their flagship Flux transcription model. Many voice agent use cases require that the agent understand and speak multiple languages. To support this, we often build complex pipelines with components that perform language detection and language switching. What we really want is for the models we use to just naturally understand multiple languages! Flux is fast, with integrated turn detection, and is the speech-to-text model in a very large number of production voice agents. This new multi-lingual support makes it even more useful.

top_tweets.text: reason #48 you should record your workshops: multiple attendees left feedback saying they wished I had given them a glossary of terms or list of definitions for things we discussed over 90 min. luckily, I recorded my audio and transcribed it all in @descript, so it took about 5 minutes to create one I prompted Claude to create a glossary using the actual language from my transcript to define terms, not it's training data, which it could do with the Descript API then had it alphabetize everything into the @NotionHQ site page with my workshop materials and sent the attendees requesting this a link straight to that block

top_tweets.author: mati
top_tweets.text: What a week in Warsaw. 2,500 leaders, builders, founders, researchers, investors and public leaders came together for the @ElevenLabs Summit in Grand Theatre. There were a lot of moments that felt very special. Poland is full of energy and optimism right now. Our researchers and builders are at the frontier of AI, and the economy keeps growing at a pace that is hard not to be excited about. It was a privilege to bring so much of the ecosystem together in Warsaw - to celebrate how far we've come, and to talk about what we can build next A few personal highlights: - Welcoming the President and Deputy Prime Minister, who shared their perspectives on innovation, security and Poland’s future - with a lot of energy and optimism - Hosting former President Lech Wałęsa and showing him our original machine - assembled by @dabkowski_piotr in his Warsaw apartment at the very beginning - Previewing our newest, most expressive models yet, and live demoing the future of customer journey when booking experiences (ending with iconic voice of Piotr Fronczewski)! - Speaking with @RBrzoska about reinventing parcel delivery in Europe, and Piotr Zak about positioning an iconic Polish company, Polsat Plus Group for the decades ahead - Announcing our partnership with @LOTPLAirlines to transform customer experience with voice agents - Introducing investors from across Europe and the US to Polish founders and builders - including all our board members - thank you @slpierre, @JenniferHli, @andrew__reed - for the first time! - Having my parents come to an ElevenLabs event for the first time Thank you to everyone who came from across Poland, Europe and further afield!

top_tweets.author: natalienkhalil
top_tweets.text: More than 7 million research papers were published in 2025. That number is about to explode. Think about all of the research agents and AI writing tools that were developed last year: @OpenAI’s Prism, @elicitorg, @FutureHouseSF, @k_dense_ai, etc. What happens as adoption of those tools inevitably increases? What happens to volume when the average researcher becomes fluent in @claudeai Code? Scientific infrastructure and peer review was designed for a different world than the one we are entering. Peer review is in crisis. (Data: DOI-indexed articles on @OpenAlex_org)

top_tweets.author: nikogrupen
top_tweets.text: Excited about this result. With @FireworksAI_HQ, we built a worker <> advisor agent. An open weight (GLM-5.1) worker delegates high-stakes / complex tasks to a closed-frontier advisor (Opus 4.7). This type of inference-time routing allows us to navigate the quality-cost Pareto frontier effectively. Lots of other cool post-training nuggets over @harvey LAB in this article, check it out!

That number represents real people using real products created by our builders. One of our biggest goals with Floot has always been to make a platform where you can build something that actually works. Seeing these numbers come together feels like proof that we’re heading in the right direction. A huge thank you to every builder who’s trusted Floot to bring their ideas to life. top_tweets.author: henryhengzj
top_tweets.text: We are joining @Workday! This is a huge step forward for our mission to make building powerful AI agents easy and accessible to everyone. @FlowiseAI started as a weekend project with a simple idea: make it easier for people to build with LLMs visually. I remembered the first line of code was written while I was on a train from Limerick to Dublin. What followed was far beyond anything I imagined: an incredible community, millions of downloads, and adoption across industries. To everyone who’s supported us along the way—thank you. You’ve helped shape what Flowise is today. I’m especially grateful to @ycombinator for believing in us, to @harjtaggar for his mentorship from day one, and to my cofounder, @ChungYauOng who’s been in the trenches with me every step of the way. We're incredibly excited to be working alongside Jerry, Peter, and the broader Workday team. We share a common vision: enabling more people to build meaningful, production-grade AI agents. Flowise isn't going anywhere - we're doubling down. With Workday’s backing, we’ll have the resources to move faster, go deeper, and continue building in the open. We're just getting started!

top_tweets.author: TamarRottShaham
top_tweets.text: What is the role of text tokens in diffusion? Do they carry anything beyond the text prompt? We study this in FLUX.2 @bfl_ml for the task of reference-guided generation, and found that text tokens hold visual information from the reference image!

top_tweets.text: Spent the whole day not coding. Instead: • Researching German nonprofits • Testing @clay vs. @LinkedIn Sales Navigator • Cleaning and structuring data in @FolkHQ CRM • Designing a as-legal-as-possible cold email process 😅 Goal: Find a scalable way to go from 5 to 50–100 nonprofit orgs. Current hypothesis: 🎯 Focus on nonprofits with 11–50 employees → Big enough to have budget → Small enough for reasonable sales cycles Less “growth hacks,” more boring, repeatable systems.

top_tweets.author: Rufus87078959
top_tweets.text: I want to say a big thank you to my longtime friend @javilopen, who built a fabulous AI tool called Magnific. The platform was later acquired by @freepik, and today, Freepik has rebranded into @magnific. In 2023, when Javilopen founded Magnific, I was one of his earliest users. Javilopen gifted me a lot of credits: I think 10,000 in value. I have since been using these credits, but when Freepik purchased Magnific, I started using Freepik more. I have not used the legacy Magnific platform in two years. Today, I decided to check the legacy platform and discovered my credits are intact. This is unheard of; there is no other company that would leave an unpaid subscription intact for two years and counting. I'm very grateful to Javilopen for building this awesome tool. If you aren't using Magnific, I highly recommend you check it out.

top_tweets.author: PQabelian
top_tweets.text: 🚨Quantum threat to #Bitcoin is becoming real! A new @Glassnode analysis estimates that nearly 10% of Bitcoin’s total supply is “structurally unsafe” in the event of a major quantum breakthrough, capable of breaking current elliptic curve cryptography. The exposed supply includes: * Early Pay-to-Public-Key (P2PK) wallets * Legacy multisig structures * Certain Taproot outputs At the same time, nearly 70% of BTC supply remains unexposed to direct quantum threats, highlighting that the ecosystem still has time to prepare, but not indefinitely. The industry conversation is evolving from theoretical risk toward practical migration paths: 🔹 Post-quantum cryptography 🔹 Quantum-safe wallet standards 🔹 Address hygiene improvements 🔹 New output formats like P2MR The broader takeaway: Quantum resilience is becoming a strategic infrastructure discussion for crypto, not just an academic one. Read more: https://t.co/RkNYIWyNWr #PQAbelian #ABEL #PostQuantum #QuantumComputing #Blockchain

top_tweets.author: FundersVC
top_tweets.text: Top AI and compute-native projects by market capitalization, cross-checked across @glassnode , @coingecko , and @CoinMarketCap ↓ @NEARProtocol (Near Protocol / Ethereum) @bittensor (Custom L1) @DfinityToday (Custom L1) @render (Solana) @AskVenice (Base) @virtualprotocol (Base / Ethereum) @GoKiteAI (Avalanche / Custom L1) @grass (Solana) @graphprotocol (Ethereum / Arbitrum) @akashnet (Cosmos) Current market data suggests that institutional liquidity in the AI and compute-native segment remains concentrated across these protocols, although exact rankings vary depending on the data provider and category methodology. What remains consistent across the major analytics platforms is a fragmented infrastructure landscape: capital is not loyal to any single ecosystem and continues to follow execution efficiency, liquidity depth, and compute availability. Rather than a single AI supercycle, the market increasingly looks like a competition between underlying networks for the base layer of the emerging machine economy. @HyperliquidX and HIP-3 should currently be viewed as an emerging infrastructure angle rather than a confirmed core leader of the AI sector, at least for now 😅

top_tweets.author: tonygentilcore
top_tweets.text: We've talked about @Glean's Enterprise Knowledge Graph since 2019. People kept asking to see it, so we built a demo. Ask Glean something like: "show me my manager's projects and the team behind them," and watch it traverse the graph in real time, mapping work and people across the org. Very cool!

top_tweets.author: jainarvind
top_tweets.text: The future of enterprise AI is giving enterprises the ability to match the right model to the right kind of work. That’s why I’m excited that @NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra is coming soon to @Glean. Nemotron 3 Ultra is a strong option for everyday work. It delivers 91% of frontier-model completeness on key metrics, with the cost profile and flexibility of an open model. As AI moves from experimentation into everyday work, cost and model choice become architecture decisions, not just product decisions. This is the broader shift I’m seeing: enterprises want the freedom to use frontier models where they matter most, and open models where they can drive scale more efficiently. They don’t want to bet the future of their AI stack on a single model family. With Glean, customers can choose from 30+ open and proprietary models and route work to the best model for the job across Assistant and Agents. Thanks to the NVIDIA team for the collaboration.

top_tweets.author: jainarvind
top_tweets.text: Can your AI answer these questions? - Who owns this customer escalation? - What changed on this project after last week's review? - Why did we decide to push the launch? @glean's Enterprise Graph connects people, projects, docs, and customers so AI can reason across how work actually gets done, instead of starting cold every time. More effective, less expensive. For a long time, people have asked us if they could actually see the graph. Now you can.

top_tweets.author: pictsbyai
top_tweets.text: Created with Gemini Nano Banana 2 on @GeminiApp Prompt 👇 A centered medium portrait of a young adult male with warm tan skin sitting casually, facing the camera with relaxed shoulders. He wears an unbuttoned warm cream-colored linen shirt, white trousers, and a thin gold chain necklace. His short dark hair is parted on the side, framing a genuinely happy, broad open-mouth smile showing his teeth, with relaxed eyebrows and a direct gaze. His hands rest organically between his thighs, loosely clasped at crotch level with fingers interlaced, his right thumb crossing over his left hand, gently resting his weight on his inner thighs. He sits squarely on a low, pristine white concrete wall featuring a smooth matte painted finish. Beyond him stretches a deep spatial background layered with a calm, pristine ocean reflecting small artificial lights from a distant shoreline. In the midground, a dark, thriving silhouette of a fan palm tree rises on the right, asymmetrically balancing the weathered, dark rocky silhouettes of breakwater pier structures dotted with small marker lights on the left. The scene captures the cheerful, energetic mood of a tropical vacation evening, bathed in a highly saturated split-complementary color palette. A vibrant sunset orange glows intensely at the horizon, smoothly transitioning into a deep navy midnight blue sky. The lighting is driven by a harsh, bright, direct frontal on-camera flash that freezes the subject, preserving stark specular highlights on his forehead, nose tip, cheeks, and lower lip. This intense directional hard light completely eliminates ambient fill, casting a deep black, harshly defined short shadow directly behind his right arm and torso against the clean white wall, drastically separating him from the twilight background. Captured in a travel lifestyle digital photography style with a direct-flash snapshot aesthetic, the straight-on perspective features a medium depth of field and tack-sharp focus with a touch of realistic digital noise, while post-processing heavily boosts contrast to enhance the flash-versus-night aesthetic, keeping the flash exposure neutral-warm and pushing the vibrant sunset oranges to maximum saturation against the deepened blues, framed in a 2:3 aspect ratio.

top_tweets.author: lilyyang169
top_tweets.text: Last Friday, @smallest_AI hosted a private dinner for community and GTM leaders building in AI-native communications - with friends from @stripe, @meetgranola, @AnthropicAI, @PLAUDAI, @rootlyhq, and @SentientAGI. What stood out wasn't just the energy in the room. It was how this group of forward-thinking leaders truly showed up and opened up within such a short time frame. At every dinner we host, we curate the guest list with intention - not just who we want to meet, but who each person in the room would genuinely want to sit across from, and why it makes sense for everyone at the table to be there. In one evening, we got to know each other, exchanged notes, and explored upcoming collaborations. The closers shared what actually moves large deals forward. The conference veterans shared which ones are worth attending - and the secrets to maximizing ROI. The event hosts aligned on future collaborations. Since community and GTM are, at their core, collaborative, one of the greatest forms of leverage in this work is the network you build. And the most meaningful connections come from rooms like this - dinners structured around genuine relationships as the foundation for what comes next. Grateful for everyone who made this room what it was. 🙏

top_tweets.author: Arshkrsingh
top_tweets.text: 🧠 Cheap LLM does the reading. Smart LLM makes the decisions. Introducing tiny-boss — a cost-saving protocol where a free worker model (e.g. @GroqInc Llama 8B) reads your full context, and a smarter supervisor (@deepseek_ai / @OpenAI) only sees the distilled answers. Result: 81–94% savings on long-context tasks. 📄 Summarize papers ✅ 🔍 Extract entities ✅ 🪵 Analyze logs ✅ Inspired by @HazyResearch minions protocol. No local GPU needed — runs entirely on cloud APIs. 📜Link to the original paper : https://t.co/iUNNuwNNJy Made in hermes-agent @Teknium @NousResearch ⭐ Try it: https://t.co/X0yNMAbcMK pip install git+https://t.co/C72ayVg9ZV #LLM #AIEngineering #OpenSource #Python #CostOptimization #BuildInPublic

top_tweets.author: DeCat2025
top_tweets.text: Prompt Drop: Alien Morning - Seedance 2.0 @Hailuo_AI Hyper-realistic cinematic sci-fi morning on an alien planet. A peaceful advanced city wakes at sunrise, filled with sleek white and silver organic buildings, curved towers, glass domes, glowing cyan details, smooth pathways, silent hover pods, red rocks, strange alien plants, and misty mountains. The inhabitants look almost human but have natural warm orange skin, subtle alien facial features, realistic expressions, and elegant everyday clothing. Show an ordinary morning routine: families leaving homes, parents going to work, children walking to school, the city slowly coming alive. 0–5 sec: wide aerial establishing shot gliding over the city as lights turn on, curved doors open, hover traffic begins, and orange-skinned families step outside. 5–10 sec: street-level tracking shot beside one family walking along a glowing curved path, parents carrying work bags, children wearing futuristic school packs, other families passing in the background. 10–15 sec: low cinematic angle near a modern alien school entrance. Parents say goodbye, children enter, workers board hover shuttles nearby. Camera rises to reveal the city fully awake. Hyper-realistic, cinematic, warm sunrise light, smooth camera movement, shallow depth of field, natural acting, detailed alien worldbuilding. Keep proportions, keep style and features, aspect ratio 16:9. Negative prompt: no text, no subtitles, no logos, no Earth buildings, no pink human skin, no cartoon style, no war, no horror, no distorted faces, no extra limbs, no chaotic crowd, no shaky camera.

top_tweets.text: Feel the music GPT Image 2 and Seedance on @higgsfield_ai Prompt : A stylish young woman with red hair walks confidently through a vibrant urban street wearing premium black-and-red over-ear headphones. Cinematic lifestyle commercial, warm golden-hour lighting, shallow depth of field, realistic city atmosphere, fashionable streetwear. She pauses, puts on the headphones, closes her eyes, and immerses herself in the music. Close-up shots of the headphones, subtle smile, hair gently moving in the breeze, synchronized with the beat. As the music builds, she starts moving naturally to the rhythm while walking through the city. Smooth tracking shots and cinematic orbit camera movements capture her energy and confidence. Nearby pedestrians notice her vibe and begin smiling. One by one, people join her, creating a spontaneous street dance moment. The crowd grows larger, dancing together through city streets and open plazas, celebrating music, connection, and joy. Dynamic camera movement, seamless transitions, slow-motion highlights, expressive reactions, energetic choreography, authentic emotions, premium advertising aesthetic. Alternating close-ups of the woman, wide shots of the growing crowd, and detailed product shots of the headphones. Music-driven editing, rhythmic cuts, cinematic lens flares, natural lighting, vibrant urban colors, ultra-realistic textures. End with a dramatic hero shot of the headphones rotating against a cinematic city backdrop, subtle audio-wave visual effects, premium reflections, luxury product-commercial styling, shallow depth of field, high-end brand campaign look, flawless commercial cinematography, 16:9 aspect ratio, photorealistic, visually stunning. top_tweets.author: Anvario
top_tweets.text: I'll be honest with you. I'm an angel investor in AI startups (got @higgsfield_ai @zeely_ai @zibraai @extellaai in my portfolio among others) and I hate AI. I hate the hype, the energy consumption, the mass hallucinations being shipped as products. Hate the way it's quietly making humans worse at thinking. So why am I building an AI company with Extella? Because the disruption already happened while nobody asked us. The decision was made by a handful of labs and a lot of venture money, and now we're all living in the aftermath. You can stand outside and be right about how bad it is. Or you can get in and try to build something that makes it less bad. I chose to get in. That's what Extella is: not "AI will save us”, more like: the damage is done, let's be smart about what comes next.

top_tweets.author: sourceforge
top_tweets.text: In this discussion, we talked with Founder & CEO of @HourOneAI, Oren Aharon. Hour One is an AI-driven video production platform that uses lifelike avatars to create personalized business messages. It offers a range of use cases, from thought leadership to training and marketing. The platform is easy to use, with a SaaS desktop app and templates available. Hour One prioritizes data security and employs encryption and regular security audits. The company serves a wide range of businesses, from CEOs using avatars for thought leadership to sales teams using avatars for customer interactions. In the near term, Hour One plans to introduce real-time interaction and cinematic use cases. In the long term, the company envisions avatars being used for communication and recreation on a large scale. Hour One dispels the myth that AI-generated videos lack authenticity, emphasizing that the tools enhance creativity and productivity for video producers.

top_tweets.author: pauliusztin_
top_tweets.text: Evaluating agentic coding systems past vibes is brutal. Yes, we can add: Tests PM reviews PR reviews CI/CD loops But how do we know the system works reliably? While speaking with @ben_burtenshaw from @huggingface at @UphillConf, he suggested a simple but powerful idea: Model your agentic coding setup as an RL environment. Not necessarily to optimize the agents... But to measure them. And I found just the tool to help with that: → https://t.co/QRwQKYY1gE OpenEnv treats agentic execution like a Gymnasium-style environment Which means your coding agents interact with environments the same way RL agents interact with games. So you can run multiple trajectories in parallel and compute metrics instead of vibe-checking one lucky run. For example: Success rate across runs Retry frequency Tool efficiency Failure patterns Time-to-completion Cost per successful trajectory The interesting part is that rewards don’t need to be optimization signals. They can simply become observability signals for your engineering process. The architecture is also surprisingly clean And it integrates with RL tooling like: TRL torchforge SkyRL Oumi Unsloth This is an important direction for agentic engineering. Agent systems need measurable evaluation harnesses. Otherwise we’re mostly just vibe-checking outputs. Check out OpenEnv here: https://t.co/QRwQKYY1gE

top_tweets.author: AlanCowen
top_tweets.text: Life update: I’m now at @GoogleDeepMind Feels surreal to not be at @hume_ai anymore, but the team there is in an incredible position to continue advancing cutting edge voice AI research. There are changes coming in how people interact with AI. There are changes coming in how labs will teach AI to make people happy. Voice intelligence is increasingly seen as a key unlock on both fronts, a thesis Hume was very early to adopt. So Hume is sharing the infrastructure for voice data, evals, and RL to help advance voice intelligence and AI empathy across the industry. It’s proving to be a much bigger opportunity than expected. top_tweets.author: zachkrall
top_tweets.text: today's my last day at @hume_ai i joined three years ago as the first design hire, helping take the product from 0→1. i worked on some of the most interesting problems i've encountered in my career: how do you design for AI that listens to tone, rhythm, and emotion? how do you build interfaces for technology that doesn't have precedent? a highlight was helping shape EVI, the empathic voice interface, the first emotionally aware conversational AI. that meant figuring out how to visualize expression data in ways that felt intuitive, designing interaction patterns for a voice agent that actually responds to how you feel. those questions pushed me to think differently about what design even is when the medium keeps shifting underneath you. i'm grateful to the hume team for building that environment together. excited for what's next!

top_tweets.author: sarick
top_tweets.text: The thing that separated @hume_ai early was the founders’ focus on semantic space theory. They kept coming back to the idea that meaning lives in how something is said - pauses, stress, tone, mood etc. This is only possible if the model is trained on real conversations, not just clean, scripted audio. AKA - data is the hard part.

top_tweets.author: richardfzhang
top_tweets.text: How to Launch Your Product on Hacker News 👇 🤔 What's Hacker News? Hacker News, by @ycombinator, is where the world's top developers share, discuss, and showcase ideas. At @InteguruAI, we've done a few HN launches leading to paying customers and great feedback. The community is full of early adopters, so a good launch means getting them to try your product or features. 👥 Who to write for? Like in any writing, knowing your audience is important. The HN community is mostly technical, but don't assume specialized knowledge in your domain. You can write in technical language, but explain details others may not know. 📰 What's a good title? Titles matter — a good title boosts clicks. Use "Show HN: ..." to signal you're showcasing something. Be succinct and direct. Show your value and uniqueness outright. For example, we titled one of our launches "Show HN: Venmo Unofficial API" for an integration that we built. Readers understood our offering right away. ✍️ What "vibe" should I go for in the main post? Hacker News isn't for constant product shilling. People are there to be entertained and learn new intellectual things. While sharing your product is important, tell how you arrived at the idea and the challenges you faced. Let your human stories shine! 🔢 What's a good structure? We don't recommend trying to game the system (you really can't). But if you're looking for a specific structure to inspire the writing process, we've found the following to help based on our experience: 1: Quickly explain your product and the problem it solves. Add a demo video. 2/3 (interchangeable): Storytelling about your journey. 2/3 (interchangeable): Real-life examples of you or others using the product. 4. Briefly mention future plans and invite the community to try the product. 🤯 Any counter-intuitive tips? While you may want to say you built the world's greatest product, being honest about current limitations is quite powerful. The HN community is full of people familiar with building from 0 to 1. They respect your attempt if you're upfront about the areas to improve. 🥵 Am I worrying too much about something? Don't stress about the post being long! When we started, we often feared we had too many words. If your post is engaging, people will finish it. However, every word, sentence, and paragraph should have real justifications for its existence. 🗣️ How to engage with comments? On launch day, expect to be at your keyboard for a handful of hours, especially if your post does well and remains on the front page for a while. Reply to comments quickly, often, and positively. HN users are good critical thinkers, so they'll post challenges. These allow you to better understand user needs. Lastly, remember to keep iterating regardless of the launch outcome, and enjoy this process!

top_tweets.author: OptibaseIO
top_tweets.text: We're live on @ProductHunt today. Over the past two years we've helped teams like @Kajabi, @riverside, @heyjasperai, @memberstack and @pheedloop run structured A/B tests. And across 3000+ teams, we kept seeing the same problems: -No hypothesis before running a test. -Tests stopped after a few days. -"Winning" results that wouldn't survive a basic stats check. Experimentation works, but only if the process behind it is sound. So we built the course we wish most teams started with. 6 modules covering: • What to test first for maximum impact. • How to calculate sample size and test duration. • How to interpret statistical significance correctly. • How to build a repeatable experimentation system. Completely free. Tool-agnostic. If you're responsible for conversion, growth, or experimentation, this might be very valuable knowledge: https://t.co/S5vcERDekt

top_tweets.author: alvarobartt
top_tweets.text: 🫢 @JinaAI_ did it again! `jina‑embeddings‑v5` w/ task‑targeted embedding distillation are out, in both small (677M) and nano (239M) variants for retrieval, text‑matching, clustering & classification! 🤏🏻 Compact models, `v5-text-small` 677M and `v5-text-nano` 239M, perfect for on-device real world applications 🏆 `v5‑text‑small` scores 71.7 avg on MTEB Eng v2 / 67.7 on MMTEB; and `v5‑text‑nano` 71.0 / 65.5, beating or matching all sub‑500M models 💡 Smarter training by mixing distillation + contrastive loss for high‑performance small models 🌍 Multilingual with support for 119+ languages 📏 Long‑context up to 32K tokens, with embeddings of 1024 dims for `v5-text-small` and 768 dims for `v5-text-nano` 🪆 Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) support for 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, and 768 for `v5-text-nano` and, also including 1024 for `v5-text-small` 🧱 Robust embeddings, stable under truncation & binary quantization 💥 Supported on Transformers + Sentence Transformers, soon on Text Embeddings Inference!

top_tweets.author: Robro612
top_tweets.text: Early in our work on @JinaAI_ -ColBERT-v2 we tried pair (1) and KD (3) training ColBERT and while each step on their own improved, pair -> KD (1 -> 3) led to instability and regression in the KD step. Seems like supervised contastive finetuning is a necessary step.

top_tweets.author: AIandDesign
top_tweets.text: I totally did it. Turned the @Kling_ai pixel clock into a lo-fi TuneShine device. Shows album covers automatically based on Apple Music now playing or with Shazam so it will even work when you’re spinning vinyl, CD or tapes. In lovely lo-fi 16x16 pixels. Claude reverse-engineered the entire BLE protocol with which iOS can talk to this thing and implemented it for me in a library I’m now using for this. Absolutely insane stuff! This stuff will all be open source so anyone with a Divoom TimeBox EVO device can play! They’re 50 bucks on Amazon. Very cool.

top_tweets.author: meh_agarwal
top_tweets.text: I'm a big big fan of open source models in Creative AI. It allows non big-tech labs to shine and build goodwill. But I'm super confused by this new set of models by @ideogram_ai (already open source) and @krea_ai (soon open source) Why are these models purely text to image and don't natively support image to image? I feel with Flux Kontext, everyone took a step forward with models to understand that professional workflows need image to image models, yet this feels a step backwards. Second, would love for image models to start benchmarking early on https://t.co/CR7PmhNOIB. top_tweets.author: ChaiWithJai
top_tweets.text: I spent this weekend setting up observability for my personal Claude Code usage with @langchain. After 100+ traces, I found something uncomfortable: Most of my "model failures" weren't model failures. They were my failures—ambiguous instructions, missing context, poor task decomposition. The discourse right now is "Anthropic nerfed Opus." But here's what I think is actually happening: Most people complaining have zero visibility into their own systems. They're debugging a black box by vibes. The nerf might be real. But you can't know until you can see your own work. @swyx has been saying AI Engineering needs the same rigor as backend engineering. He's right—and observability is the gap. We went from "it works on my machine" to "it works in my chat window" and called it production. That's not engineering. That's demo-driven development. @hwchase17's LangSmith showed me exactly where my prompts broke down—which steps lost context, which instructions were ambiguous, which tasks I was asking the model to do that I hadn't actually specified. Turns out I was blaming the model for my own sloppy engineering. Before you tweet about the nerf, check your traces.

top_tweets.author: cloudxdev
top_tweets.text: Must watch video from @langchain + @ManusAI , key points and a TLDR: 1. Context Rot: Agents need tools, but more tool usage = more context = degraded performance. Context Engineering means showing the model only what's needed for the next immediate step. 2. Core Techniques: - Offload (token-heavy data to filesystem) - Reduce (prune old info) - Compact (reversibly strip reconstructable data) - Summarize (irreversible compression) - Retrieve (on-demand via search) - Isolate (sub-agents with separate context). 3. Don't Fine-Tune Early: Startups should avoid specialized models pre-PMF. General models + context engineering = faster iteration. Model iteration speed caps product innovation. 4. Manus Strategy, Two-stage: - Compact first(reversible, e.g., remove file content but keep path). Only Summarize when compaction fails. - Use structured outputs (schemas) for summaries, never free-form. Always retain recent tool calls for behavior. 5. Multi-Agent Patterns: - Communicating: Sub-agent starts fresh. Best for low-context tasks (e.g., find code). - Sharing Memory: Sub-agent sees full history, different prompt/tools. Best for complex dependencies (e.g., Deep Research). 6. Layered Action Space: Prevent tool overload: - Atomic: 10-20 core functions (read, write, shell, browser) - Sandbox Utilities: Pre-installed CLI tools (converters, linters) - Packages/APIs: Python scripts with pre-authorized keys for heavy compute 7. Evaluation: Public benchmarks (GAIA) ≠ real user preferences. Gold standard: user ratings (1-5) on completed sessions. Use execution tests (can it finish the job in sandbox?) over Q&A. Human review for subjective quality (e.g., visual appeal). Conclusion: Over-engineering is the enemy. Biggest performance gains come from removing, not adding complexity. Make the model's job simpler, not harder.

top_tweets.author: itsclelia
top_tweets.text: Coding agents might feel like magic, but they aren’t 🪄 That’s exactly why I’ve been eager lately to understand how they work under the hood, using the most natural approach I could think of: observability. I built fuseproxy, a @langfuse-instrumented API proxy that coding agents can call instead of talking directly to provider servers, giving me visibility into usage, costs, and everything flowing in and out of the agent. I also built instrument-claude, an observability tool that turns Claude Code's hook events into OTel-compatible traces, spans, and events, providing an overview of everything happening within a conversation turn or across an entire session. I wrote about both of these approaches to coding agent observability in my latest blog post: https://t.co/QpOkJRZHH5 Hope you enjoy the read, and I’d love to hear your thoughts👇

top_tweets.author: jerryjliu0
top_tweets.text: As of yesterday, we made everyone within @llama_index research/engineering/product a Member of Technical Staff. Yes all the frontier labs have done this for years. But we didn’t only make these changes for performative reasons 🙂. The concept of a technical role within our company has fundamentally changed with the rise of AI + coding agents: 1️⃣ Eng, research, and product are collapsing into one role. Now that coding/project management are commoditized, every engineer is expected to own e2e outcomes and also “know more things” across the stack, from evaluating/iterating on core models to shipping scalable, reliable backend services to iterating on core product. 2️⃣ We are fundamentally building a technical product - our mission is to provide the highest-quality document processing platform for AI agents. This involves extensive research into both core model capabilities and agentic harnesses, and integrating it into an easy-to-use product for both AI startups and enterprise. 3️⃣ Simon and I believe in transparency, flat organizational structure, extreme autonomy combined with collaboration, grit, and the ability to ship blazingly fast. This is not for everybody, but when it works, it creates an amazing culture of velocity and trust. Our careers page still lists more specialized roles across AI/backend/infra etc. which we will still use for calibration, but the reality is also that you’d likely be working very cross-functionally across the org.

top_tweets.author: ConorBronsdon
top_tweets.text: Sometimes you need to start over. But that decision is hard. @llama_index had to make that call: they built one of the most popular AI frameworks in the world, but saw the agent harness and frontier labs coming for them. So they drilled down on a durable moat: extracting enterprise data, and providing the best, most accurate context. @jerryjliu0 joined me on the @chain_ofthought to talk about how they iterated, why they disrupted their own product before a frontier lab could, and how LlamaIndex became the best in the world at turning messy PDFs and PowerPoints into context enterprises can actually leverage for their agentic workflows. As Jerry put it: pointing Opus 4.7 or GPT 5.5 at your document corpus feels like 80% accuracy in a demo, but that's a deception. The other 20% hallucinates a number or misreads a table, and your whole agent workflow breaks. In legal, insurance, and finance the real bar is 95% plus. That's what LlamaIndex is focused on today - watch the full, frank conversation with Jerry below👇 Chapters: 0:00 Is the AI framework era over? 1:56 What died and what survived 6:31 Why context quality is the moat 8:12 Defining the context layer 13:18 Coding and vision as the abstraction layer 18:13 The bet that context compounds 23:59 Which verticals are adopting 25:14 Why 95%+ accuracy is the real bar 29:49 The file system as an agent primitive 34:33 Surviving your own pivot 37:15 Reinventing strategy and hiring 42:00 Agent memory as persistent context 44:41 Model personalities and cultural memory 47:51 Writing with AI 50:19 Closing thoughts

top_tweets.author: jerryjliu0
top_tweets.text: Last I checked, the @llama_index OSS framework integrates with 78 (!!) vector stores. Investors (and builders starting out, and honestly me for a while) deemed this space as commoditized. One of the lessons I've learned that's especially relevant here is that in a commoditized market, regardless of initial distribution, the best product will still win. It takes a certain level of obsessive focus, a contrarian bet on technology (optimizing cost through object storage) to pull this off, but this is a great example that it can absolutely be done. Huge congrats to @turbopuffer 🫡 big inspiration for us on our own parsing product

top_tweets.author: jerryjliu0
top_tweets.text: We're excited to be an official shoutout at the Google I/O Developer Keynote 🔥 @llama_index is building the document infrastructure for AI agents, and we plan to integrate even more heavily with both the model layer (Gemini API) and agent harness layer (Antigravity agents) to support all developers within the Google ecosystem.

top_tweets.author: ConorBronsdon
top_tweets.text: Jerry Liu built one of the most installed pieces of AI plumbing of the last three years. Then he sat down and told me the framework era he helped create is over. The agent harness ate the abstraction layer. The patterns @llama_index used to wrap (query rewriting, reasoning loops, provider glue) have solidified into Claude Code, OpenClaw, and Manus. The abstraction moved up, out of Python classes and into natural-language skills and MCP tools. So what survives? Context quality. And Jerry bet the company on it, looking to become the best company in the world at extracting data and context locked in PDFs - drilling down and focusing to beat out the frontier labs. In my latest @chain_ofthought, @jerryjliu0 (co-founder & CEO of LlamaIndex) breaks down: → Why Jerry believes the context layer is a durable moat, not the scaffolding → Why 95%+ accuracy is the real bar in legal, insurance, and finance, and why pointing Opus or GPT at a million PDFs gets you ~80% → The pivot from open-source framework to managed document infrastructure (LlamaParse, LlamaCloud) → How you survive disrupting your own product while companies 1,000x your size try to do it to you Thank you to Jerry for a frank, fascinating conversation! 🎧 Listen on any app: https://t.co/7SsNlVVOxj 🎬 Watch: https://t.co/a5DOMQmrPJ

top_tweets.author: DataChaz
top_tweets.text: 🚨 @LLAMA_INDEX JUST CREATED THE WORLD'S MOST VERSATILE OPEN-SOURCE PARSING TOOL Building document-heavy AI has always come with friction. Most parsers force a choice. Stay fast and local but deal with brittle, broken formatting, or get structural accuracy by constantly pinging cloud LLMs. LiteParse v2 refuses that trade-off entirely. It's a library that rewrites the rules of local ingestion. By rebuilding the entire engine in Rust, LiteParse v2 is officially the fastest parser available. A 100MB, 457-page PDF runs in an unbelievable 0.777 seconds 🔥 But what makes it truly remarkable is its insane flexibility across any stack. The LlamaIndex team wanted to build a single tool that could handle every step of your local pipeline: - Deploy Anywhere: → You can run the exact same, insanely fast library in a Python backend, a Node server, or entirely in the browser via WASM. - See Everything: → Beyond fast text extraction, it allows you to generate specific page screenshots so your multimodal agents can "see" what text alone misses. - Parse Anything: → It doesn't just read PDFs. It hooks into LibreOffice and ImageMagick to automatically convert and parse 50+ formats, from .docx and .xlsx to .png and .tiff. LiteParse v2 is completely free, Apache 2.0 open-source, and 100% local. Cloud models are great when you hit incredibly dense tables (that's exactly why LlamaIndex built LlamaParse for the cloud), ... but I think basic document ingestion should be fast, local, and FREE. Give your Vision LLMs exactly what they need with one simple command: `lit screenshot document.pdf --target-pages "1,3,5" -o ./screenshots` Open-source repo in 🧵 ↓

top_tweets.author: tmophoto
top_tweets.text: I think i figured out why @lmstudio was half the speed of llama.cpp with the 35b MTP model. @lmstudiodevs quick question: is there a way to run LM Studio with a CUDA 13 llama.cpp runtime on Windows? In my Runtime menu, the newest option I see is CUDA 12 llama.cpp v2.16.0. My standalone llama.cpp benchmark used b9186 CUDA 13.1 and was 2x as fast with MTP 35b model. I’m trying to do a cleaner apples-to-apples comparison and i want to keep using lm studio. Is there a CUDA 13 runtime extension pack available, or a way to point LM Studio at a custom llama.cpp build?

top_tweets.author: DouglasGan
top_tweets.text: One piece of feedback keeps coming up: “Why aren’t you using AI agents to automate earning strategies?” We’ve also heard from many FX-exposed investors asking for something simple enough that they can plug into @claudeai, @ManusAI, or @perplexity_ai and deploy with minimal effort. So, we’re building something even simpler than https://t.co/yzIITXgEDx Soon, users will be able to simply park liquidity and allow it to be utilized across PSPs, neobanks, on/off ramps, remittance providers, marketplaces, and more. Many assumed yields would be small. Turns out, 8–15% for safer currencies and up to 500%+ for exotics. Looks pretty attractive to me. My savings account gives me less than 0.5% annually. Banks ask you to jump through hoops too: credit your salary, set up recurring spend, spend a minimum amount every month, and more. With significantly more effort, maybe that 0.5% becomes 3-4% at best. Money should work for me, not the other way around.

top_tweets.author: iashwinikumaar
top_tweets.text: Perplexity is best. Most people won’t agree: I’ve used various AIs for basic tasks, and I think Perplexity AI (@perplexity_ai) stands out compared to xAI (@xai), ChatGPT (@ChatGPTapp), and Microsoft Copilot (@MSFTCopilot). Here’s why: •Text Copying: Perplexity lets you select copy text directly from result, a feature missing in its competitors. •Search Results: When you search in Perplexity, it displays results with images, similar to a Google search. This makes it very convenient. •Intuitive App: The app’s design offers quick access and feels highly user-friendly. •Modern Aesthetic: Its logo and overall design are fresh, unique, and modern, while the others tend to follow a similar, less distinctive style. • Analysing big excel files: Other failed at this This opinion is based on daily tasks and regular queries that most people encounter. For heavy users tackling complex tasks, preferences might differ. Overall, I find Perplexity to be the best option.

top_tweets.author: StatueofIBBertY
top_tweets.text: @MistralAI continues to win in our benchmarks - I think it is by far and away the most accurate, cost effective, and time performant model for automation based tasks. For Cotera's customer use cases, it beats out every other model, including frontier models. I think Mistral might not be *as* performant on the use cases that are common today - the ones with a 1 to 1 interaction (code agent, chat assistant) However, I think the way that we use AI today is going to be a very very small fraction of how it's used in the future (background). I am still ASTOUNDED that Mistral has such a small part of the existing mindshare.

top_tweets.author: iiiwokk
top_tweets.text: How do you build a brain? Everyone's talking about world models and unified enterprise data that agents and people can reason against but I haven't seen one live. So we built it at @NotionHQ, on Notion! We built a lot of AI agents on the People Team but quickly hit a wall. An agent is only as capable as the data it can reach and without people data, HR agents can't do much. But anyone in this space knows how difficult it is to make people data AI-ready. There's a reason why Workday isn't in Claude today. We've been thinking about this problem space for a while and here's how we're experimenting. Here's an onboarding demo. While seemingly simple, an onboarding agent in Notion is actually pulling from a single source of (governed & permissioned) data to personalize each new hires experience and sending the manager automated updates in Slack. We're using Human Intelligence's MCP here, pulling dummy data from Workday + Ashby. It's super cool bc unlocking people data means having a truly unified data model. We can pull business data. Work context. All this will power the company of the future, and I'm excited we get to push the boundaries 😊

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: I just talked to my CRM. That's it. Said "create a deal" on a call. @octolane AI CRM did the rest, picked the right stage, added the primary contact, pulled in the extra stakeholder, swept in a year of history, ran the calcs, and built out full activity signals + enriched data The CRM that fills itself out 🚗

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: Something happened in our @octolane AI CRM today that I want to share, because I don't think people realize what we've built yet. A new user signed up for Octolane. Within minutes, our own instance of Octolane: → Read the signup → Decided the question worth asking was "is this a YC company?" → Created that field itself → Wrote an ICP summary for the account → Did the same across all 1,205 records in our self-serve pipeline I did not create the "is YC?" column. I did not write a prompt. I did not tell it what our ICP was. Octolane read our sales conversations, figured out that YC batch mattered to us, and built the schema around it. Respan came back YES W24. Paradigm, YES W24. Korso, YES S26. Kapsule, NO - and a one-line summary of what they actually do. Every single value has a source. Every source has been verified by a second agent. This is what self-driving CRM means. Every other tool is still asking you to map fields. If you're a founder reading this, this is the bar now. If you're a VC reading this, ask your portfolio companies if their CRM is generating its own fields yet.

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: third coffee in. just staring at this table. Last touch. Next meeting. First email. Last email - Every column, automatically filled in, by itself thanks to @octolane - Self-driving AI CRM Most founders i talk to are still out here manually updating last contacted. typing notes after every meeting. dragging cards. That sounds more like homework. octolane reads gmail. reads calendar. bi-directional sync actually doing the work, we did a lot of work to get this right. Octolane also suggests the next move before i even think to ask. - prep for next call - draft a follow-up - suggest a next move - research the company one tap. all of it reading the account status and telling me what to actually do. even the live graphs at the bottom feel alive. 24,993 accounts. numbers breathing in real time. I felt bad writing this. somewhere a founder is manually updating "last contacted" right now.

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: woke up and @octolane had written new ai memory blocks about how i work, without me asking. - how i sign off investor updates - when i offer my personal cell in support emails - how i respond to competitor questions on calls - the footer i append to every AI-drafted email - my writing tone when handling negative feedback i taught it none of this. it watched me work and figured it out. the CRM you have to feed every day is dead. self-driving is the only thing that makes sense.

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: I just asked @octolane: "Find the last 11 signups and write onboarding emails." 60 sec later, I had all 11 email draft, personalized and in my voice! what happened under the hood: 1. Octolane pulled every new signup automatically. 2. Looked up each company. Matched the industry to tailor the messaging. 3. Then wrote each email the way I actually write 4. CRM updated in the process. This would normally take me 20+ minutes of tab-switching, copy-pasting, and rewriting. Instead I just ask Octolane and it did the work end to end. And based on the response rate it will automatically improve the email drafts for future responses. Just incredible!

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: I work way more hours now thanks to Claude Code and Codex. Like last night, I was doing a complex sentiment analysis AI skill creation for @octolane where AI CRM will analyze every customer touchpoint and suggest me deal state automatically to focus on what deals I should prioritize. Coding itself took less than 30m. But fixing every single edge case and deciding how to surface it to users took almost all night. The bottleneck moved and now it's me. I remember that writing code used to be the expensive part. AI definitely killed that cost. What's left is the part that was always hard but hidden behind keyboards: "Taste" What breaks at the edges and what users feel when they see it. AI did compress execution but It didn't compress thinking for us. So the future is not gonna be fewer hours. It's gonna be denser ones. Engineers and founders become decision compressors, more calls per day, where each one mattering more, none of them automatable. So the edge in 2026 isn't gonna be how fast you ship, It's gonna be "Judgment at speed" 1. are you shipping the right thing, for the right users, at the right time? 2. doesn't matter if it's 2pm or 2am, can you make decisions fast and make them well?

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: Watched a customer spend 6 hrs straight in @octolane today. Years of building AI CRM taught us one thing worth saving: 1. There's a specific number that unlocks activation 2. There's a specific feature set + specific numbers that unlocks retention When you find both of them, there's no going back. But how did we find ours? 🚀 Activation: Look at every user who converted to paid. What's that one behavior they all did in week 1 that churned users didn't? That's gonna be your activation event. Now your job to find the smallest number of repetitions that predicts conversion. 🔄 Retention: Research your month 2 retained users. What combination of features did they use in weeks 2 to 4 that churned users skipped? That's your retention game will surface. Sounds obvious but I promise you It's not gonna be when you're in it. We cracked activation first. Then retention. Both took painful iteration, shipping, breaking, rebuilding, shipping again. It was an excruciating process for us where we were not sure if we're getting closer or just running in circles. Until suddenly you are, and you can't unsee it. Teams are now migrating from HubSpot, Pipedrive, and Attio to Octolane. We did zero outbound and all word of mouth. Because the agent-first economy lets us solve the edges incumbents can't. Founders, what number unlocked activation for you?

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: A Salesforce customer just signed to switch to @octolane this morning. They just connected their Salesforce to Octolane, and Octolane did the rest: 1. Found where revenue was leaking 2. Identified 300+ missed follow-ups and drafted every single one 3. Built all the automations they needed 4. Enriched their entire dataset 5. Created a sales brain inside Octolane Now I have 5 more sales call to go today, and the research is already done by Octolane. Attached photo: new customer case study dropping soon!

top_tweets.author: coffeewithone
top_tweets.text: fastest way to use @octolane is just talking to it. it's incredible seeing unbelievable uses cases customers use Octolane for: A customer asked Octolane to audit their existing CRM and clean up the pipeline. It flagged stale deals, duplicate contacts, opportunities sitting in the wrong stage and then fixed them. Then he generated a full pipeline report by pulling from 4 different data sources at once: deals, email activity, meeting notes, and enrichment data stitched into one narrative. zero csv back and forth. Then he started generating meeting prep in bulk with every call on the calendar that month. Dream scenario that we keep perfecting day after day is that I'll just describe the outcome and it will take care of the rest, and it's working. The best CRM is no CRM
