top_tweets.author: saniaspeaks_
top_tweets.text: Created with GPT image 2 on @openart_ai Prompt: Transform the uploaded image into a retro Windows MS Paint outfit-builder interface aesthetic. Keep the full-body cutout of the subject centered on a clean white canvas, as if placed inside an old Windows XP / MS Paint workspace. Layout details: Left side: classic MS Paint toolbar with pixel-style icons and old UI elements. A vertical column of floating wardrobe/accessory slots (hair, top, bag, pants, shoes, etc.) shown as detachable outfit pieces. Bottom: a pastel color palette bar similar to old Paint color swatches. Window title bar at top with simple retro naming (e.g. “untitled - Paint”). Subject appears like a digital paper doll / dress-up character. Visual style: Early-2000s internet aesthetic Slight low-resolution softness Airbrushed + scanned magazine feel Soft glow around the subject Tiny sparkle highlights Muted pastel colors with one or two saturated accent colors Minimal shadows Cute, playful, nostalgic UI composition Image treatment: Preserve pose and clothing identity from the original image Cutout edges slightly softened Keep proportions elongated and editorial White negative space for a clean composition Subtle JPEG compression / vintage screen texture Keywords: MS Paint UI, Windows XP aesthetic, digital dress-up game, Y2K nostalgia, paper doll fashion, retro desktop, kawaii editorial, internetcore, soft glow, scrapbook UI, fashion collage.Watermark: Add a subtle watermark in the bottom-right corner of the image. The text should be vertical (rotated 90°) and read “VNCNTAI”. Use Montserrat font, white color with low opacity (semi-transparent), medium size.

top_tweets.author: AIwithkhan
top_tweets.text: The story of a day protected beautifully GPT Image 2 and Seedance on @openart_ai Prompt : Ultra-realistic luxury skincare commercial storyboard, 12 cinematic frames arranged in a professional director's storyboard sheet. The story follows one woman throughout a full day, showing how invisible sun protection quietly supports her life without her noticing it. Frame 1: Early morning. Woman opens curtains. Soft sunlight floods a modern apartment. Frame 2: She waters indoor plants while sunlight hits her face naturally. Frame 3: Close-up of sunlight passing through leaves, creating moving shadows on healthy skin. Frame 4: Minimalist bathroom. She applies sunscreen effortlessly while checking her daily schedule. Frame 5: Macro shot of cream disappearing instantly into skin. Frame 6: Busy city crossing. She walks confidently among morning commuters. Frame 7: Outdoor café meeting. Friends laugh around a table under bright daylight. Frame 8: Rooftop lunch. Strong sunlight overhead, but she remains comfortable and confident. Frame 9: Afternoon bicycle ride through tree-lined streets. Frame 10: Golden-hour portrait. Natural skin texture, no makeup focus, authentic beauty. Frame 11: Sunset reflection in a train window. Long day completed. Frame 12: Product hero shot with tagline: “Protection So Invisible, You Forget It's There.” Premium French pharmacy aesthetic, warm sunlight storytelling, realistic skin texture, visible pores, cinematic 35mm photography, director annotations, camera arrows, shot descriptions, luxury commercial pitch-board design, Apple advertisement quality, A24 cinematography mood, sophisticated visual narrative, ultra-realistic storyboard sheet, 8K.

top_tweets.author: SimplyAnnisa
top_tweets.text: Created with seedance 2.0 on @openart_ai One continuous take, no cuts. Single flowing follow-cam oner. Ultra-realistic cinematic style. Follow cam locked behind the skater as she drops into a steep San Francisco-style street, the city stretching downhill before her. Wind rushes past as she rapidly gains speed. The camera stays behind her, racing downhill. She ollies over a parked car, then grinds a long handrail down a steep staircase, sparks flying beneath the board. Cable-car tracks and colorful row houses blur past at high speed. The chase camera swoops lower as she launches off a cresting hill, soaring over an intersection with the bay visible in the distance. She lands smoothly and carves sharply through the street. The camera gradually pushes closer, still in a single uninterrupted shot, as she rides along a banked corner and accelerates toward the camera. The golden-hour sun creates warm lens flares and dramatic highlights behind her. Epic cinematic orchestral score with powerful percussion, realistic skateboard wheel sounds, rushing wind, subtle city ambience, and environmental audio. No spoken dialogue. Photorealistic visuals, cinematic action-film aesthetic, natural lighting, realistic shadows and reflections, high-detail textures, smooth camera motion, stable image, HDR, 2K resolution, sharp focus, no flicker, no ghosting.

top_tweets.author: _odsc
top_tweets.text: 🎙️In this episode, we speak with @rajistics, an Agentic AI Engineer at @OpenHandsDev, about the agentic harness behind modern AI systems, which entails the retrieval, memory, tools, orchestration, and execution environment that determine whether an AI agent can work reliably in practice. Rajiv shares practical insights on context engineering, agentic RAG, memory design, orchestration patterns, and the tradeoffs teams need to understand when building reliable AI agents. 🎧 Listen to the full episode here → https://t.co/qsRAuCedcK

top_tweets.author: zebanderson
top_tweets.text: I needed a transcript from a YouTube video for a presentation, so I googled “free YouTube transcript generator.” I’ve historically trusted the @otter_ai brand, so I clicked through. After OAuth signup and ~7 onboarding screens, it was still unclear how to actually get a transcript. I went back to the original page, clicked around, tried multiple chat flows, and ended up with the result in this screenshot. This kind of UX dark pattern is an instant dealbreaker for me. Part of my job is recommending AI platforms for industry use. You can have the best transcription tech in the world, but if the growth strategy depends on trapping users in confusing onboarding flows, I won’t recommend it. What you do with a little, you do with a lot.

top_tweets.author: TheTuringPost
top_tweets.text: Recently @pinecone introduced Nexus – a new knowledge-engine layer for AI agents that reduces token use by up to 90%. It’s built on top of a vector database, but shifts reasoning earlier in the pipeline: from retrieval at query time to knowledge compilation before the agent even asks for information. Instead of giving agents raw files, Nexus prepares task-optimized representations in advance. These representations include artifacts – structured forms of information tailored to each agent’s specialization and workflow. For example, a finance agent gets only billing schedules, pricing rules, and usage thresholds. Pinecone’s VP of Product Jeff Zhu explains it like this: "The artifact representation can range from markdown files to extracted entities to tables. These artifacts are then indexed for retrieval within Pinecone’s database which support both semantic, sparse, and full text search capabilities so that the right artifacts are retrieved during query time and then composed for the final structured output response from Nexus." So vector databases are evolving from passive storage layers into active knowledge engines for agents. Read this to learn what distinguishes vector database in the Agentic Era: https://t.co/B20aFqw7Yd

Two high-speed flight craft skim across the surface layer between islands, one white with blue energy lines and one dark graphite with orange magnetic pulses, weaving through shifting terrain that rises and sinks unpredictably. SHOT 2 The white craft slips between two massive floating landplates as they slide apart. The dark craft follows through the narrowing gap. Camera tilts and rolls as entire islands rotate like gears around them. SHOT 3 They drop into a low corridor formed by stacked magnetic debris fields, where floating metal fragments orbit like storms. The camera stays tight, threading through spinning structures and drifting industrial ruins held together by invisible force fields. SHOT 4 Inside a transparent control sphere embedded in a floating island, a lone operator floats weightless, watching as the craft streak past outside. Reflections ripple across the curved glass as islands rotate around the capsule. SHOT 5 The craft ascend through a vertical magnetic lift zone where islands align into a spiral staircase in mid-air. Camera corkscrews upward with them. SHOT 6 They exit above the magnetic sea, revealing thousands of floating islands slowly rotating in synchronized orbital motion across the horizon. #pixversecpp

top_tweets.author: HLS_dev
top_tweets.text: I had a lot of fun building props and structures using @tripoai for the #vibejam. Here’s the workflow I ended up using between agent prompting: @poe_platform (3 tabs setup): Tab 1: Generates consistent image-style prompts for different items. I’d request something like “a crafting armor bench” and it would reliably output prompts in a consistent visual style. This helped maintain cohesion across assets. Tab 2 & 3: Run the same prompt through both Nano-Banana and Nano-Banana Pro simultaneously, then pick whichever result looked best. @tripoai workflow: Take the selected image and import it directly into Tripo’s Smart Mesh. Generate a model targeting roughly 1K–5K polygons depending on the asset type. @blockbench cleanup: The biggest recurring issue was pivots and rotations. Not every model needed adjustments, but weapons and structural assets almost always required pivot correction for proper in-engine behavior. That was basically it. I was able to handle both modeling and coding in parallel. Game development is getting wild right now. Some footage of the models/images generated through Tripo follows below. #threejs #gamedev

top_tweets.author: Predicti0r
top_tweets.text: TRENDING: FIFA WORLD CUP 2026 NATION TO REACH ROUND OF 16 My best risk/reward NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c This market on @Polymarket is not the same as to qualify from the group. To reach the Round of 16, a team must: > get out of the group > reach the Round of 32 > win one knockout match That makes the new World Cup format extremely important: > 48 teams > 12 groups > top 2 from every group qualify > 8 best third place teams also qualify > then comes a new Round of 32 The market can easily overprice teams that look capable of qualifying from the group. But qualification is not enough. A team can finish 2nd or 3rd, reach the Round of 32, and still get a terrible matchup. So for NO on I'm looking for teams with: > difficult group path > low chance to win the group > dangerous Round of 32 bracket if they finish 2nd > weak or limited attacking ceiling > important injuries or squad problems > too much dependency on one game state > not enough quality to win a knockout match That is why I like Scotland NO, Sweden NO and Iran NO. 1) Scotland NO for 78c Group C: Brazil Morocco Scotland Haiti This is one of my favorite NO spots. Scotland can absolutely fight for qualification from the group. But they need to win a knockout match. And that is where the problem starts. Why: Scotland are probably fighting for 2nd or 3rd place. Brazil are clearly stronger. Morocco are also stronger in tournament profile, athleticism, technical quality and recent World Cup experience. So Scotland realistic routes are: > finish 2nd behind Brazil > finish 3rd and hope to qualify > very unlikely win the group Scenario A: Scotland finish 2nd in Group C This is the best realistic scenario. But it is still not great. C2 plays F1. It means Scotland could face the winner of Group F. Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia So if Scotland finish 2nd, they probably get Netherlands, Japan or Sweden in the Round of 32. That is a very difficult matchup. Netherlands have far more individual quality. Japan have better technical structure and tournament discipline. Sweden have more attacking power with Isak and Gyokeres. Winning that knockout match is a very different task. Scenario B: Scotland finish 3rd in Group C If Scotland beat Haiti and maybe get a draw against Morocco, they can be alive. But 3rd place path is much less controllable. They can qualify, but then the bracket can throw them into a strong group winner. For NO, this is exactly what we want. Scotland might survive the group but still get a terrible Round of 32 opponent. Scotland summary: Scotland can qualify from the group. But this market requires more. They need to win a Round of 32 match. Their realistic 2nd-place route likely sends them into F1. Their 3rd-place route is unpredictable. The Billy Gilmour injury is also important. That makes Scotland NO for 78c one of the cleanest risk/reward options. 2) Sweden NO for 77c Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia This is a bracket risk NO. Sweden are not a weak team. Actually, they have a very dangerous attacking profile. Alexander Isak, Viktor Gyokeres, Anthony Elanga Dejan Kulusevski would normally be a major creator, but his absence is a big problem Sweden route is very dangerous if they do not win Group F. Why: Group F is very uncomfortable. Netherlands are the favorite. Japan are disciplined, technical and very hard to beat. So Sweden have a real chance to finish 2nd or 3rd. But that is exactly where the bracket becomes ugly. Scenario A: Sweden win Group F Very unlikely This is the bad scenario for NO. F1 plays C2. That could be Morocco or Scotland. In that case, Sweden would be very live. If Sweden win the group, YES becomes much stronger. But I do not see Sweden winning Group F as the base case. They need to finish above Netherlands and Japan. That is possible, but not the most likely outcome. Scenario B: Sweden finish 2nd in Group F This is the key NO thesis. F2 plays C1. And C1 is probably Brazil. That is the problem. If Sweden finish 2nd, they may run directly into Brazil in the Round of 32. That completely changes the market. Sweden can beat many teams. But Brazil in a knockout match is a very different level of difficulty. Scenario C: Sweden finish 3rd This is also rather good for NO. A 3rd place team can qualify, but the Round of 32 opponent becomes less predictable and usually harder. Sweden would lose control of their route. Sweden summary: Sweden are dangerous. But they probably need to win Group F to make YES truly strong. If they finish 2nd, they may get Brazil immediately. If they finish 3rd, the bracket becomes random and difficult. They have great forwards, but the path is dangerous. That makes Sweden NO for 77c a strong bracket risk play. 3) Iran NO for 83c Group G: Belgium Egypt Iran New Zealand Iran NO is the safest profile of the three. Not the biggest upside. But very logical. They need to survive the group and then win a Round of 32 match. Why Iran NO makes sense: Belgium are clearly stronger. Egypt have Salah and Marmoush. Scenario A: Iran finish 2nd in Group G G2 plays D2. Group D: USA Turkiye Paraguay Australia That means Iran could face USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. None of these matchups are easy. USA at home would be very difficult. Turkiye have far more technical and attacking quality. So even if Iran finish 2nd, their Round of 32 path is not comfortable. Scenario B: Iran finish 3rd Iran can maybe qualify as one of the best third place teams. But then the bracket becomes much worse. A 3rd place path usually means facing a group winner. For a team with limited attacking upside, that is bad. Iran summary: Iran can be uncomfortable. They can fight for 2nd. They can maybe reach the Round of 32. But to reach the Round of 16, they need to win a knockout match. Their G2 path likely gives them USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. Their 3rd place path is unpredictable. Their attack is limited without Azmoun. That makes Iran NO for 83c a strong conservative NO. Final summary: The new World Cup format makes NO markets very interesting. Because many teams can qualify from the group. But not all of them are good bets to reach the Round of 16. The key is the Round of 32. My best NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c @PolymarketTrade @PolymarketSport

top_tweets.author: omarsar0
top_tweets.text: I highly recommend this. The Agentic Review is a new podcast from @QodoAI hosted by Itamar Friedman and Nnenna Ndukwe, and it's a great AI coding show that's neither hype nor doom. It's honest conversations about what shipping high-quality AI-generated code actually looks like. We need more of these conversations around AI agents. As an AI engineer, I think about this stuff constantly. A few things make this show worth your time: * It's a conversation about what good code means in the era of coding agents. * The hosts actually push back at guests instead of doing softball interviews. * The current guest lineup is strong: @dexhorthy, @shanselman, and @fullstackpython. I work a lot on context engineering, so the Dexter Horthy episode resonates the most for me. His take on context engineering as one of the biggest moats right now matches exactly what I'm seeing in production. He talks about a five-month experiment where his team stopped reading the code, then ripped it all out and rebuilt it by hand. That lesson about owning your context and actually reading what your agents produce is something every AI engineer needs to understand today. The bigger thesis across episodes: typing code may be dying, but the SDLC, code review, and craft matter more than ever. AI sprinkled on a broken software lifecycle is "a band-aid on cancer" (Hanselman's line, and it stuck with me). Thanks, @QodoAI, for the partnership on this post.

top_tweets.author: itamar_mar
top_tweets.text: This week, we shipped @QodoAI Findings. It's the natural next step from PR-by-PR review. Here's the problem it solves. If you manage an engineering team right now, you can probably answer a question about any single pull request your team shipped this week. What you likely cannot answer, at least not quickly, is the version of that question that spans your whole org. > How many issues were caught and fixed across our repos this month? > Which services are accumulating the most risk? > Is review quality on the payments team improving or sliding? > Are the same issues getting flagged for the same reasons over and over? These are the questions that come up in staff meetings, even in board prep these days, in conversations with security... Answering them used to mean stitching together Slack threads, asking around, or pulling a spreadsheet by hand. Qodo Findings, consolidates every issue Qodo has surfaced across your reviewed pull requests into one view. Three parts that work together. A top row of analytics: total critical findings, percentage resolved, average findings per PR. Volume, throughput, trend. The high-level read without having to open anything. Filters underneath: by repo, by owner, by issue type. Cut the data the way you actually need it. Pull up only security findings. Look at a single team. Focus on one service. A full searchable, sortable list of every issue, with the ability to drill straight into the underlying PR when something stands out. Available now in beta on GitHub, GitLab, BitBucket, and Azure DevOps. Would love to get any feedback even DM me! Taking a step back. The point isn't a new dashboard. The point is that the review layer has to operate at a different altitude now. PR-by-PR is necessary, but it isn't sufficient. When code is being generated continuously across teams and repos, leaders need to see across teams and repos. The same way they see across any other part of their operation. This is the first release in that direction. More coming. We care about the software health as a whole, not only PR-by-PR, and there is a lot of innovation needed here. Read more: https://t.co/S6aqusTIg6

top_tweets.author: ApexImperialist
top_tweets.text: I started using @TheQuillBot instead of Grammarly since the latter degraded the quality of their free version. QuillBot allows me to tether my writings to specific versions of English (UK, US, AU, etc.), & what I've noticed is that my English is a mixture of UK & US, confusing the AI's checker ... I'm not sure how interesting that is to others, but I find it kind of interesting & I assume it is the result of having learnt British English from my dad & from school, while having interacted with Americans for a long time. But this might signify that the various versions of English might mix up due to the internet, leading to a more spectrum-defined version of it that accepts different types of spelling in the future.

top_tweets.author: timsemelin
top_tweets.text: 🔥@ReadyPlayerMe has been acquired by Netflix. The company started over a decade ago as a small avatar experiment by young builders from Estonia. What began as a niche idea around digital identity has grown into a widely used avatar platform across games and virtual worlds. As part of the acquisition, the Ready Player Me team will join Netflix’s gaming division to work on cross game avatar technology. The focus is on enabling players to carry their identity and fandom seamlessly across different games and experiences. The journey involved years of experimentation, failed ideas, rapid learning, and close collaboration with thousands of developers. That process ultimately shaped Ready Player Me into a scalable product with global adoption. The acquisition aligns with a long held vision of open avatars that are not locked into a single game or platform. With Netflix’s scale and reach, that vision now has the opportunity to reach a much broader audience. This move marks another step in Netflix’s expansion into gaming and interactive experiences.

top_tweets.author: AsafNadler
top_tweets.text: Show me your calendar, I’ll show you why you’re stuck. Founders hit a wall when they try to scale with old responsibilities shackled to their feet. This weekend, I did a 1-hr audit using ChatGPT + @reclaimai to map how I actually spend time vs. how I should. Here’s what I found: 1.I wear all the hats: founder, C-level, mid-mgmt, hands-on. The first two cannot be delegated, but 80% of the rest can and should be. 2.I spend lots of time on thought leadership & hiring. Formally, they’re not “mandatory,” but it reflects my values and moves the needle. 3.I actively make space to learn: from advisors, partners, and reading. This is critical for balancing short and long term. 4.The more I invest in mid-management, the more they want to step up, and the more I can. That’s the best time investment long term. Highly recommend this exercise. 1 hour can unlock your next level.

top_tweets.author: wangyuanzju
top_tweets.text: Quick comments on ChatGPT Atlas Today, OpenAI launched its own AI browser, ChatGPT Atlas, which highlights its abundant resources and restless energy. However, ChatGPT Atlas shows little innovation — it’s roughly equivalent to Dia plus @remio_ai 1.0. We introduced browser memories in remio seven months ago, and we believe that this data belongs to the user. Therefore, we store all of it locally on the user’s computer, rather than doing what OpenAI does — keeping the data on their cloud servers and not allowing users to export it. Data belongs to the user and should be freely processed with whichever large model is most suitable. Users have this right — any attempt to forcibly bind user data to a specific model can only stem from capital interests, not user interests.

top_tweets.author: NIftek
top_tweets.text: For years, I had ideas in my head that I couldn’t actually build. And I think that’s where the future of EdTech gets really exciting: when the people closest to the problem can build the solution. A special thank you to the Replit Edu team for having me on today. top_tweets.author: NickSpisak_
top_tweets.text: Here's how I built a voice agent to do discovery calls. Her name is Annie. She is our assistant. She has a phone number on the @retellai platform which is backed by @twilio. Annie picks up the phone, talks to a prospect for about 15 minutes, and a fully written-up transcript is the final output. This is what's happening under the hood, in six steps. 1. The call comes in Annie picks up in about a second. Her voice is ElevenLabs Cimo, which sounds like a real person who's glad you called, not a robot reading a script. Her brain is GPT-5.2. I used 5.2 as the model that got the job done. A good balance between intelligence and cost. 2. She checks if you should even be on the call Before she says hi, the system looks up your phone number to make sure you actually booked an assessment. → No assessment on file ... she politely says goodbye → Assessment on file ... she uses one credit and moves on. This keeps our costs manageable. 3. She introduces herself Thirty seconds, tops. She says her name, tells you the call will take about 15 minutes, confirms who you are and what you do, and asks if you're ready to start. Sounds small. It's not. Setting the frame upfront is the difference between a focused call and a rambling one. 4. She listens. Wide angle. Five minutes of broad discovery. What's your business. What's your role. What tools do you use every day. Where does the friction live. She's told to listen, not diagnose. Most humans jump to solutions in minute two. Annie doesn't. She just learns your world and quietly clocks your top pain points. 5. She goes deep on the two biggest pain points Now she zeroes in. Five to seven minutes on the top two things she heard. How many hours a week does this cost you? What would "better" actually look like? She picks two and goes hard, instead of skimming ten. This is where the call earns its keep. By the end, we know exactly where AI would move the needle and roughly what it's worth in time. 6. She wraps up and writes the transcript analysis Annie gives you a plain-English recap of what she heard. Sets clear next steps. Ends the call. Then GPT-5.2 takes the transcript and pulls it apart into structured data: → Top pain points → Hours wasted per week → Tools in play → Sentiment Analysis The data is sent via webhook where we kick off an AI Tools Assessment skill that we custom wrote. That's how we tactically use AI. You don't need to build a rocket ship... Just know how to connect a couple pieces together and solve a pain point or two...

top_tweets.author: HenrM_Cruz
top_tweets.text: A few thoughts after seeing this announcement blow up and building the @RowsHQ AI Analyst for the past 3 years: 1. It reinforces the idea that spreadsheets aren't going anywhere. It's the only common product where any businessperson can look at and analyze data. You saw this with Claude, the Ramp labs release, Shortcut, and many others. Frontier labs are realizing this as well. 2. LLMs will go after the application layer. You can use a chat interface to ask questions, write copy, or draft an email, but the end result happens in a productivity tool somewhere else. This "somewhere else" is where collaboration happens, where (very) high margins live and where you don't need to worry about inference costs or hallucinations. 3. Spreadsheets are almost code, that's why this works. 4. Data analysis agents are world-class when they can handoff spreadsheet formulas to Python when needed. We learned that last year when we added support for Python in @RowsHQ AI Analyst. The agent automatically decides if it can solve the request using spreadsheet formulas (easier for 1B people to understand), or if it needs Python. 5. For this to ultimately replace most spreadsheet use, it needs a System 1 - System 2 type system, so that easy requests are handled very fast (routed to simpler models with less context), and hard problems take as much time as they need. That's how we architected the AI Analyst, which results in System 1 tasks taking a median of 6s (e.g. add a column with profit) and System 2 tasks taking 69s. It won't matter how smart it is at creating a DCF if it takes 30 seconds to format a range of cells. Most knowledge work is editing existing work, not creating new one. 6. The next step-function improvement is having your data analyst agent accessible everywhere you work. This means opening and analyzing CSVs from your email inbox, reading pdf receipts, answering questions on Slack, etc. 7. If you really want to see what a best-in-class spreadsheet agent can do, try https://t.co/lndfMNCbZC.

top_tweets.author: HenrM_Cruz
top_tweets.text: This is how I use the AI Analyst in @RowsHQ to go over the website traffic to my book review blog and summarize it for me. "Do an analysis of my GA4 website traffic from October: website visitors, top source mediums, most visited pages. Compare with last October, and add a few charts and a text summary." This is a glimpse of how business teams will interact with their data: use AI to fetch your business data, analyze it, surface the results and allow you to audit the results and create a beautiful document that you can share with your team.

top_tweets.author: HenrM_Cruz
top_tweets.text: Hearing from @nearestnabors on local models at @lisbonai_ We’re currently working with a large customer on deploying @RowsHQ AI Analyst on top of local models and the trade-offs are clear: - Local: cheaper, works offline, great for task specific jobs. - Cloud: current models are just much smarter and can do a lot more. It does seem clear that as you project fwd the capabilities of SMLs, in 1-2 years we’ll start seeing large AI applications on top of local models.

Image 👉 Multi-View (GPT Image 2, SeedVR2 upscaling, Background Removal) 👉 High-poly (@tripoai 3.1) 👉 Remeshing (@TencentHunyuan Polygen) 👉 UV Unwrapping (Tencent) 👉 Retexturing (Tripo, Meshy, @Microsoft Trellis 2). Done. One single platform. One API/MCP. End-to-end. 🚀

top_tweets.author: emmanuel_2m
top_tweets.text: That was one seriously heavy workflow, made for a 35-second concept ad. The final output was built 100% in @Scenario_gg + @cursor_ai (Scenario MCP). No video editing software involved. 80+ nodes, multiple models, including: - Gemini 3.1 (images) - Seedance 2.0 (video) - ElevenLabs (TTS + Music Advanced) - Magnific (video upscaling) - Photoroom (background removal) - some utility nodes stitching everything together.

top_tweets.author: emmanuel_2m

top_tweets.author: emmanuel_2m
top_tweets.text: This @Scenario_gg Workflow takes any image (character, object, or scene) and automatically extracts all individual elements (typically 10–15) in one single click. It use an LLM node to identify & list each element, then SAM 3.1 from @Meta to mask them, @OpenAI GPT Image 2 + Pixa background removal to reconstruct and isolate. From there, you can group everything into a grid or keep iterating (rearranging elements, more image editing, 3D conversion, or video gen...)

top_tweets.author: tecoker
top_tweets.text: Had a great chat with Harry Kim from @nvidia , @philipkiely from @baseten , and Arya Asemanfar from @SierraPlatform, for a salon dinner on long-running agents and inference. We were joined by 20+ technical leaders from @Replit , @nvidia , @hippocraticai , @PrimeIntellect , @anyscalecompute , @togethercompute, @thinkymachines, @netflix , @tryramp , @MemGPT and more. Consistent themes that emerged through several conversations: - Nobody owns the agent harness yet. App teams are building it by default, inference platforms want to absorb it from below. Sentiment pointed to the application layer as its home — though some platforms are getting close. - Evals are the precondition for everything else — model right-sizing, routing decisions, fine-tuning confidence. Nothing off the shelf is specific or good enough to work generally. Everyone is handling it in-house. - The next wave for inference is real-time: video, audio, multimodal. Beyond that, inference-time compute and observability are the areas people are focused on. - VR as a longer-term area of interest, when the technology can serve the capabilities consumers actually need. Super energizing to chop it up with some of the best in the business! cc @felicis

top_tweets.author: IsraelJIL
top_tweets.text: I JUST WORKED WITH A VERY NICE AI AGENT! Today I integrated an AI agent, called Cole, I Connected cole to my work space on @SlackHQ, asking it to debug my code which we have been on for a while now, it audited the codes done by myself and my team. So far, While we keep working and shipping, it does the corrections for us, now we don't have to upload to claude for audit and code corrections we just use work directly on Slack alongside, Cole. TIMES HAVE CHANGED! we all know those feeling with hiring teams and assistants to manage portfolio, data, codes, communities, I think that era is over. meet: @PocodotAI's AI Agent called: COLE What is @PocodotAI all about? well, here are some interesting facts you need to know about cole or https://t.co/HDjcuqCyZn WHAT IS COLE? Cole is Pocodot's AI Chief of Staff - an orchestrating agent that breaks down complex tasks, creates plans, delegates subtasks to specialized agents, and monitors progress on your behalf. Cole also works alongside other Agents/AI, they are called: TEAMS Teams are groups of agents that work together on multi-step workflows. Each agent's output feeds into the next agent's input, creating an automated pipeline. Now Cole can be integrated with other socials or work spaces to coordinate workflows and help you catch up with past events that took place in your chats. I just tried it and its cool. Next connection would be to my @telegram and @WhatsApp to help me harness all my past activities. More importantly, remind me of things I'd miss. would keep you updated on this, also creating a video, showing the steps to connecting and working with Agent cole. #builtwithcole.

We have voice AI founders, engineers and researchers from DeepMind, Meta, NVIDIA, Stanford and Harvard joining us alongside the top VCs and enterprise leaders in the Bay area. 📍 Menlo Park, Palo Alto 📷 June 8, 5:00–8:00 PM Speakers: → Arjun Jain, Chief Scientist at https://t.co/Ka9yGGsMxd (PhD, Max Planck; worked with Yann LeCun at NYU; Adjunct Faculty, IISc) Talk: The Speech-to-Speech Landscape – half-duplex, full-duplex, and what the architectural choices actually mean for real conversational AI. → Sameer Khurana, Lead AI Researcher at https://t.co/Ka9yGGsMxd (PhD, MIT CSAIL Spoken Language Systems; research at MERL and Ultravox AI) Talk: From Audio Language Models to Conversational World Models – building the next generation of voice agents from first principles. top_tweets.author: ainativedev
top_tweets.text: .@Sourcegraph analysed 1,281 coding agent runs across large enterprise codebases and found the bottleneck often wasn’t the model — it was context retrieval and navigation. Agents struggled once codebases grew large enough: pulling in the wrong files, thrashing between tools, and making locally correct changes that broke systems elsewhere. One stat says a lot: a task that took one agent 96 tool calls and 84 minutes dropped to five calls and under five minutes with better retrieval infrastructure. The takeaway is this: coding agents need far better context and navigation layers around them to operate reliably in production. 🔗 Read the full story here: https://t.co/LsatesrRDC

top_tweets.author: Yura__eth
top_tweets.text: this new AI can generate and edit audio faster than you think @StabilityAI just dropped Stable Audio 3 it’s a family of open-weight audio models for music and sound effects, with editing support built in the interesting part is not just generation, but variable-length output and inpainting, so you can create and modify audio more naturally 🔸the architecture is doing a lot of work here >> a compressed audio autoencoder called SAME turns stereo 44.1 kHz audio into compact latents, which makes longer generation much more efficient >> then a diffusion transformer works on those latents, conditioned on text, duration, and masks for editing 🔸what stands out most is the practical focus >> Stable Audio 3 is designed so inference cost scales with duration, instead of forcing everything through one fixed maximum length >> that makes it more usable for real workflows, especially if you care about longer clips or iterative editing 🔸stability AI also pushed the training pipeline pretty far >> they used flow matching, distillation warmup, and adversarial post-training to get fast generation without relying on classifier-free guidance at inference >> that means fewer steps, less overhead, and a more efficient generation loop 🔸the lineup is pretty clear too: small, medium, and large models >> small and medium weights are available openly, while large is enterprise-only >> for most people, the main takeaway is simple: this is a serious step forward for audio generation and editing, not just another demo

top_tweets.author: madzadev
top_tweets.text: Step 3.7 Flash is a Flash model for real agent workflows. It’s fast and cost-efficient, but the part I care about is whether it can take a messy input and turn it into something actually usable. So I built ClipBrief. @StepFun_ai The idea is simple: upload a video, sample frames across the timeline, send them straight to Step 3.7 Flash, and get back a clean brief. No extra OCR step. No separate vision module glued on top. I tested it on a 6-minute YouTube explainer I hadn’t watched before. It picked up what was actually on screen — the article title, the diagram, the shift in the story — and turned that into a TL;DR plus timestamped key points. What I liked is that it didn’t stop at “this video is about X.” It gave me a structured readout of the clip: what happened, roughly when it happened, and how the main points connected. This is where Step 3.7 Flash makes sense to me. Not as a model that just answers one prompt quickly, but as something that can hold a small workflow together — read the frames, understand the context, organize the output — while staying quick and cheap enough to run often. I’d use this for lectures, meeting recordings, tutorials, product demos, or any video I want to skim as notes before deciding whether to watch the full thing. If you’re building AI agents, coding workflows, search-powered apps, or multimodal tools, Step 3.7 Flash is worth trying: https://t.co/2vfV8t7Zzb #sponsored #ad

top_tweets.author: AppenResearch
top_tweets.text: @AppenResearch independently evaluated @subquadratic's SSA kernel - a learned sparse attention mechanism designed to reduce the quadratic scaling limitations of full attention. Results at 1M-token context lengths: - 56.2× wall clock speedup vs. FA2 - 62.8× FLOP reduction (validated via torch.profiler, <4% variance from theoretical) - 95.6% average score across RULER tasks at 128K - 86.2% average score on the hardest MRCR 8-needle bucket (512K–1M contexts) - 81.8% SWE-Bench Verified resolved rate Full report: https://t.co/9OpvaqDPbt

top_tweets.author: layerlens_ai
top_tweets.text: LayerLens is partnering with @subquadratic to evaluate SubQ on Stratix. 🔍 Same benchmark suite every model goes through: reasoning, code generation, instruction following, tool use, and long-context 📊 Results published in Stratix Public alongside 200+ models including GPT 5.5 and Opus 4.7 🧬 First model on Stratix built on a non-transformer attention architecture 🔄 Stratix Enterprise for continuous evaluation across future SubQ releases SubQ uses Subquadratic Sparse Attention (SSA), an alternative to traditional transformer attention. SSA claims to massively increase context processing without sacrificing performance. A massive claim that has sparked a ton of debate. Transparency, openness, and auditability. top_tweets.author: alexvoica
top_tweets.text: Today, I walked past the Old Street roundabout on my way to a SXSW London side event, and saw @synthesiaIO on a billboard with some of the best companies Europe has ever produced. I'll admit it gave me a much-needed energy boost ahead of speaking on yet another panel about AI's problems. The billboard is part of #BuiltinEurope, a campaign @balderton has launched to tell the truth about what is happening right now in European tech. The story we've heard for years has been one of catching up. Europe could lead, the headlines said, if only it had fixed this or that. That's because building in Europe was treated as a thing you had to overcome. Now, for the first time in a long while, the opposite is true. Met with indifference at best and opposition at worst at home, European founders are forced to build companies that work everywhere from the first day, and they're doing it with style (and substance). At Synthesia we feel this in our bones. We were built in London and we have been global from the start, because being European made that the only sensible way to think. So if you have ever thought about building a company in Europe, or joining one, consider this your invitation. https://t.co/cvFiwU9dG3 is now live, with a jobs platform pulling together open roles from a thousand European startups, and a directory of the continent's best incubators for anyone ready to start something of their own. Europe is building. Come build with us.

top_tweets.author: NinaDSchick
top_tweets.text: More great news from @synthesiaIO - one of the most innovative AI companies!

top_tweets.author: alexvoica
top_tweets.text: To be clear, @synthesiaIO is proud to be part of Built in Europe and the case @balderton makes is the right one. I am less comfortable when that ambition gets folded or confused with the version of sovereignty taking shape in Brussels, where the instinct is to reduce reliance on foreign technology through procurement rules, subsidies, and a thickening layer of complex and overlapping regulation. Real sovereignty comes from building generational companies strong enough to win on open ground. Shielding them from competition only swaps one dependence for another and creates inferior products and services at higher costs. We need more of the former, and less of the latter.

top_tweets.author: alexvoica
top_tweets.text: Today, @synthesiaIO's security team is announcing a new multi-agent system that allows us to keep pace with the high volume of AI-generated code shipping through our codebase: https://t.co/8prHK0qa9D As more companies use AI to write their software, they will inadvertently create security flaws which can then be exploited by bad faith hackers equipped with powerful AI models. Claude Mythos is expected to help with this issue, but the model is currently available only to select Anthropic partners, not the general public. Therefore, we are sharing the recipe for our tool so we can help any company approach Mythos-levels of security performance today, with off-the-shelf models that are generally available. Additionally, our recipe achieves this performance at a reduced cost, meaning it can be used by larger and smaller companies alike. @mtschammer, head of security at Synthesia, spoke with @natrubio__ from @theDeepView about the design decisions we've made and how this project came to life: https://t.co/TvhbwiYbqW

top_tweets.author: amuldotexe
top_tweets.text: SOP driven problem solving for using LLM tools like @windsurf_ai @cursor_ai @tabnine - based on 350+ hours of playing with them over the last few months - Create a new folder for context retention docs called aa_ref_01 - Create file named r01_initial_ideation.txt for just noting down all the ideas without any structure - Create file named r02_domain_analysis.txt which will contain the best of the conversation we have with tab nine or @web in order summarize the best domain knowledge needed for the problem statement - Create file named r03_prd_v1.txt which will be the first PRD for the user journey at a high level - Create file named r04_prd_v2.txt which hopefully is a more precise version of the previous PRD based on multiple iterations on the previous file - Create file named r05_arch_L1.txt which will contain the first attempt at the architecture of the PRD - this file should leverage a relationship of consistency with r04_prd_v2.txt + r02_domain_analysis.txt PAUSE HERE & NOTICE: a. LLMs should be treated as search + assimilation tools, you have to bias them with right keywords b. LLMs need to iterate to arrive at a more refined output c. LLMs need to sumarize information which will lost by the sliding context window, hence you constantly create new docs which are in sync with previous docs now - Create file named r06_arch_L2.txt which will contain the second iteration of the architecture of the PRD - this file should leverage a relationship of consistency with r05_arch_L1.txt + r04_prd_v2.txt + r02_domain_analysis.txt - Create file named r07_explain_architecture.txt which will contain the explanation of the architecture of the PRD - this file should leverage a relationship of consistency with r06_arch_L2.txt + r04_prd_v2.txt + r02_domain_analysis.txt. This file is basically a rubber-duck debugging of the architecture with us as the listener who wants to make sure that the r06_arch_L2.txt is well thought out - Create a file named r08_TDD_v1.txt which will basically ask the LLM to write a TDD plan to implement the r06_arch_L2.txt in the most minimalistic way possible, it should be consistent with r04_prd_v2.txt & r02_domain_analysis.txt - If the r08_TDD_v1.txt is good enough, then select each step of the TDD plan as a checkbox - and ask the strong LLM (code LLM) to implement the TDD plan in the most idiomatic bug-free way possible line by line, thoroughly following the TDD methodology of starting with Red Test, then Green Test, then Refactor - Create a file named r09_bug_log.txt to note copy all the bugs encountered in the process of implementing the TDD plan - Create a file named r10_avoid_bugs.txt which will contain meta patterns and best practices discovered during the implementation of the TDD plan - Create UAT plan for the whole feature, ideally this should be very close to r04_prd_v2.txt - and manually check the results, and document them in r11_uat_results.txt - Create a file named r99_unclassified.txt to note down all the prompts, ideas, mistakes, lack of knowledge or references or links which you encounter during the journey with timestamps PAUSE HERE & NOTICE: d. LLMs need to be asked to reason themselves so they can find their own logical fallacies, like rubber duck debugging e. LLMs need to be given reference files for patterns to avoid, be it via past bugs or anti patterns to avoid f. LLMs need to follow TDD because it makes it easier to arrive at the final code - it is an easier prediction problem if you know what will be the requisite final pattern, also helps you avoid mistakes early enough g. LLM whispering is a cumulative skill so constantly document your prompts, steps & learnings p.s. most won't be able to follow the above steps and would rather smash "fix this" every 2 minutes because they lack the patience to follow these steps methodically

top_tweets.author: iPhelipeVR
top_tweets.text: A wave is coming. And nothing will stop it. Artificial intelligence no longer just answers questions. Now it writes code. And more and more, it executes it. The giants are already on the battlefield: @OpenAI, @github, @AnthropicAI, @CursorAI, @Tabnine — Generate code, but don’t execute or host it. @Google, @awscloud, @replit, @cognition_labs Generate, execute, and host the code — but within centralized cages. @dfinity with #Caffeine, built on #InternetComputer — Generates, executes, and hosts the code entirely on-chain. No servers. No permissions. No owners. This wave is inevitable. But only one path leads to true technological sovereignty. The rest… are just more efficient versions of the same prison. On June 3rd, for the first time, intelligence will run free. To the entire #ICPCommunity and our global reserve army: It’s time to leave the trenches and ready the memes. The countdown to #CaffeineDay has begun. #InternetComputer #SovereignAI #SelfWritingInternet #CaffeineAI #DDay #InDomWeTrust

top_tweets.author: amuldotexe
top_tweets.text: It's half past midnight since 2 pm I've been trying to get a postgres x go x react app to run on docker in a way such that it can deployed on railway @grok @tabnine @cursor_ai helped I did get the docker to run on my system but not on railway so the day ended in a mild disappointment I spent the first 2 hours doing something just with Cloudflare DNS manager trying to configure cmake for Google sites - and failed at it miserably I think I didn't zoom out for almost 8+ hours not a good sign - should've taken a break should've asked for help also, but somehow my ego won't let me ask for help from friends why can't I figure it out myself - the ego asks but it's a far better day than what I had expected it to be - at least I put in a few hours of focus - yes heavily cortisol driven, but still I gained a lot of familiarity there's a headache, the brain feels numb, but there's a sated joy - because you remember - you are still the teenager who'd obsess on a problem and lose a day on it of all things I learnt in Kota 20 years ago, this was the most useful skill: the ability to sit on a problem alone this lonely work is the difficult part - the LLMs help, but they don't quite solve everything, you still gotta keep talking to them, nudging them should've taken more breaks, but this is far better than not putting in the hours in the struggle maybe the adhd meds have started showing results on the focus side also or maybe it's too late & I can't think clearly either ways there's only one insight that our only job is to slow down time slow down time that's all we can do slow down time iykyk p.s. evening tea was good today

top_tweets.author: MiniMax_AI
top_tweets.text: We're going LIVE tomorrow with @togethercompute 🔥. @zpysky1125 is pulling back the curtain on M3: sparse attention, 1M context, all of it. You don't want to miss this.

top_tweets.author: MiniMax_AI
top_tweets.text: We wrapped a live session on M3 yesterday with the @togethercompute team & our researchers @zpysky1125 and @HaohaiSun A few highlights 🧵 1. MSA (MiniMax Sparse Attention) is the star ⭐️. Unlike CSA/HCA, which compress the KV cache, MSA keeps the real, uncompressed KV and does block-level selection with a small top-K. That's how the 1M context window stays tractable. 2. The efficiency win is huge. In our previous generation, ~30% of per-decode wall-clock time went to the attention kernel. With MSA that now drops to ~5%. Big gains for long-context generation. 3. M3 isn't just a coding model. Natively multimodal (image + video in), ability to handle long-horizon agentic tasks, and even operate a desktop computer. People are already throwing game-dev + Minecraft-style builds at it (Unity included) and it's holding its own. 4. M3 can self-evaluate on vision-coding tasks: it builds a website or SVG, browses and inspects its own rendered output, judges it, and iterates - grading work visually. 5. We're also seeing junior-analyst-level performance on finance tasks; something we haven't even showcased publicly yet. 6. What's next: harder long-horizon / multi-file tasks in future releases, scaling data + post-training (RL) compute toward pre-training scale, and going deeper into finance, legal & bio. Thanks to everyone who joined 🙏 Try M3 link in the comments👇

top_tweets.author: SpenserFX
top_tweets.text: The new @tripoai TripoSplat on the right (image to splat model), and then my custom image2splat on the left. Mine is 30 min ea for more of a hero asset look, custom splat count, custom baked lighting, full spherical harmonics.. Tripo is actually quite fast, no harmonics, and fairly blurry texture with some stronger hallucinations on geo.. Can be a really nice tool for background scene dressings and if you're using something like DLSS5 in the future maybe it's perfectly poised to enhance, BUT if you don't mind 30 min and have a 5090+ my Image2Splat is pretty awesome!

top_tweets.author: degensing
top_tweets.text: VoiceMoat vs Typefully A beautiful editor won't fix a generic voice. @Typefully is the best thread composer in the AI Twitter category. Not "good for an AI tool." Good by any standard. Keyboard shortcuts, thread blocks, split view, multi-platform publishing. @VoiceMoat is a voice model trained on your actual posts. Auden learns your 9 voice dimensions, generates drafts in your specific style, scores every output 0-100 against your profile. Both tools live in the X creator stack. Both have serious users. Both produce publishable content. Which is why the comparison keeps coming up. They solve different bottlenecks. A creator with Typefully but no voice model has a beautiful editor producing generic-AI drafts. The writing surface is great. The output sounds like everyone else using AI on X. A creator with VoiceMoat but no thread composer has voice-accurate AI in a functional interface. The output sounds like them. The composing experience is fine but not elegant. The right answer for most serious creators is both. When budget only allows one, the question is which problem is louder right now: If the writing surface itself is slow and fragmented.. or you publish to 5 platforms simultaneously.. or your voice is already strong and AI tools already sound like you.. Typefully. If AI drafts feel generic and your audience is giving you "did you use AI?" signals.. or you live in replies and need in-feed drafting.. or you want to measure voice drift over time.. VoiceMoat. The editor question and the voice question are separate. Know which one you're actually hitting.

top_tweets.author: Eli5defi
top_tweets.text: Distribution is king, and that’s never been more true for content creators. If you’re a creator using @typefully and @claudeai, you’re leaving a lot on the table if you’re not using this 3-step method to optimize your posts: - Set up your Typefully MPC in Claude - Ask Claude to run an analytical review of your post using the prompt below - Review and take action as per suggestion --- You are an expert Social Media Strategist and Data Analyst. Objective: Perform a comprehensive performance audit of my [Platform Name, e.g., X, LinkedIn, Threads] account using my exported analytics data from Typefully MCP. Provide a strategy that balances growth with time-efficiency. → Content DNA & ROI The “Stickiness” Factor: Identify which content categories (e.g., Educational, Personal Stories, News Updates, etc.) have the highest Save/Bookmark-to-Reach ratio. Which posts are people keeping for later vs. just scrolling past? The “Show More” Trigger: For long-form text or threads, analyze the hook (the first 2 lines). Which opening styles consistently get users to click and read the full post? Media vs. Message: Compare the performance of text-only posts vs. posts with images/video. Is the extra effort of creating visuals resulting in a proportional increase in Profile Visits or Link Clicks? → Time & Velocity Mapping The High-Engagement Window: Pinpoint the 60-minute windows when my audience is most “reactive.” Don’t just give me a time—explain the Engagement Velocity (how fast interactions happen in the first 30 minutes) and how it affects overall reach. The “Quiet Hour” Opportunity: Are there times when engagement is lower but reach is high? Identify whether I should post during off-peak hours to face less competition in the feed. → Repost/Retweet Spacing Strategy The Frequency Audit: I currently repost my own content at [Insert your intervals, e.g., 1hr - 6hr - 12hr]. Based on the data, is this causing “feed fatigue” (the same people seeing the post too many times), or is it successfully reaching new time zones? The 24-Hour Flywheel: Design an optimized repost schedule that covers three distinct global regions (e.g., morning in the US, EU, and Asia) without overlapping on a single user’s timeline more than once every 8 hours. → Conversion & Growth Funnel Follower Attribution: Which specific posts actually drove New Follows? Distinguish between "Viral Hits" (high views/low follows) and "Community Builders" (low views/high follows). Bio & Call-to-Action (CTA) Check: Based on the spikes in profile visits, suggest 3 ways to optimize my Bio or Pinned Post to convert more visitors into long-term followers. ---

top_tweets.author: Aj_Krish23
top_tweets.text: This week I helped write this Notebook on converting Documents to Stylized HTML using @UnstructuredIO 's VLM based Partitioning Strategy. this parser feels really interesting to me because unlike a lot of the parsers that output json or markdown, the Vision Language Model here outputs a HTML of every page. The reason for html is also based on a simple intuition: if you delete the original source, does your parsed output still contain everything you need? It’s also my hunch that since these Large Language Models have primarily seen a lot of web-scale data, hence making them talk in HTML would be more familiar to them compared to a different language. Check out the notebook here: https://t.co/SviYAHCokQ

top_tweets.author: BoysClubWorld
top_tweets.text: A pattern we've noticed on Show Me Your Stack: The best builders have a system for catching ideas before they disappear. @vercel founder, @rauchg showed @julesrosenberg Timeless, an internal tool he uses to capture bugs, product feedback, screenshots, screen recordings, and quick thoughts while he's in the middle of working. A few use cases he shared: > recording bugs the moment they happen > capturing UI feedback without writing a long ticket > saving product ideas before they disappear > sharing context with teammates asynchronously > creating a running backlog from real-world usage The common thread: less time documenting, more time noticing. What's interesting is how much of product development comes down to collecting observations before they're forgotten.

top_tweets.author: ycombinator
top_tweets.text: .@Veriff is the identity layer for the internet: matching people to government-issued IDs with a selfie so companies can verify users instantly and stop fraud at global scale. top_tweets.author: patloeber

top_tweets.author: victorialslocum
top_tweets.text: 𝗧𝗲𝘅𝘁. 𝗜𝗺𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀. 𝗩𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗼. 𝗔𝘂𝗱𝗶𝗼. 𝗣𝗗𝗙𝘀. One embedding model. One unified space. @googleaidevs just released 𝗚𝗲𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗶 𝗘𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗱𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝟮, their first fully multimodal embedding model - and it's now available in @weaviate_io. The model maps text, images, videos, audio, and PDFs into a 𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝘂𝗻𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗲𝗱 𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗱𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗰𝗲. This means you can query with text and retrieve relevant videos, or search with an image and find related documents, or any other combination - all using the same model. It supports: • Text (up to 8,192 tokens, 100+ languages) • Images (up to 6 per request, PNG/JPEG) • Video (up to 120 seconds, MP4/MOV) • Audio (native ingestion, no transcription needed) • PDFs (up to 6 pages, embedded directly) The model also uses Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL), so you can scale dimensions down from the default 3072 to 1536 or 768 depending on your performance vs. storage tradeoff. It's already available in Weaviate, and I've included a walkthrough in the video below of building a 𝗺𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗮𝗹 𝗣𝗗𝗙 𝗥𝗔𝗚 𝗽𝗶𝗽𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲. We embed each PDF page as an image using Gemini Embedding 2, add it to Weaviate, then query with text to retrieve relevant PDF page images. These images are passed to Gemini Flash to generate answers using the document context. The dataset has "needles" hidden in the documents - so when we ask "what's the secret flower?", the pipeline needs to use multimodal understanding of both text and images to answer correctly. Check out the model release blog: https://t.co/azI0Ee2lHs PDF RAG notebook: https://t.co/C8hHnhaGUl

top_tweets.author: victorialslocum
top_tweets.text: 𝗨𝗻𝗽𝗼𝗽𝘂𝗹𝗮𝗿 𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗼𝗻: The best hires aren't experienced. They're hungry. And we're searching for two of them right now. We have two open positions in the Growth team at @weaviate_io: 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘄𝘁𝗵 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿 We're looking for people who aren't afraid to work hard, who want to grind, and who are hungry to learn how to define narratives and win on social media. Not people who check boxes. Not people who want a comfortable 9-5. We want the next diamonds - people who see an opportunity to learn from a team that's built a developer community from the ground up and think "I want to know how they did that." You'll learn: • How to craft narratives that cut through noise • What actually works on social media (not what the courses tell you) • How to build genuine engagement with developer communities • The difference between marketing 𝘢𝘵 developers and marketing 𝘸𝘪𝘵ℎ them This isn't going to be easy. But if you're the type of person who gets excited by that statement rather than intimidated by it, you might be exactly who we're looking for. Apply here: https://t.co/v0lU0QfX7B Complete the challenge. Impress us. 💎

top_tweets.author: CShorten30
top_tweets.text: Vector search looks easy, until you hit massive scale! I am super excited to share a new episode of the Weaviate Podcast with Başak Eskili from @bookingcom on production-scale vector search, RAG, and agentic AI with @weaviate_io! 🎙️💚 The podcast begins by discussing Booking's tipping point into adopting vector search and emerging use cases. 🚀 The scale of Partner-to-Guest messaging alone is insane! There are nearly 250,000 such exchanges *daily*, and Booking's Agent is already helping with 10s of thousands of these! 🤖💬 Başak describes how the team navigated increasing scale and workload complexity. They ran an exhaustive evaluation of Weaviate with 100M embeddings and tests often left out of common ANN benchmarks. This includes Filtered Vector Search, Multi-Threaded Concurrency, and testing with simultaneous Reads and Writes. 🎛️ The podcast concludes with Başak's career journey to Booking and her thoughts on Travel Agents! 🧳 This is an awesome one. I'm extremely proud of the Weaviate team for this success, and very grateful to Başak for sharing this story on the podcast. 💚 Links below! 👇

top_tweets.author: Arindam_1729
top_tweets.text: Most AI coding agents are bad at vector DB queries. They write code that looks right, but the retrieval strategy is wrong (semantic vs hybrid, filters, limits, schema assumptions). @weaviate_io Agent Skills fixes this. It gives the agent structured guidance for: • schema inspection • ingestion patterns • semantic + hybrid search • query planning So tools like Claude Code generate RAG pipelines you can actually ship, not vibes-based queries. Result: faster builds + far fewer broken retrieval systems.

top_tweets.author: RalconStudio
top_tweets.text: Here’s my experience as a @webflow template creator so far. Just sharing my honest thoughts based on what I've experienced. And yes, I might be a little biased since we also create Framer templates. The biggest positive for me is that template reviews are much faster. But honestly, the rest has been pretty stressful. Our first template got approved, but it never appeared on the marketplace. Since we were a new creator, we had to contact support so they could manually make it visible. Then we wanted to edit the template page, but there was a bug that completely prevented us from accessing the edit page. We reached out to support, and they fixed it. Later, our second template got approved. When we tried to edit that one, the exact same thing happened again. The template edit page was blank, and once again we had to contact support to get it fixed. Most recently, Webflow introduced a requirement to install the Webflow Validator before submitting templates. We installed it, but when we tried to submit, we got an error saying custom scripts were detected that didn't match approved GSAP patterns. So now I'm back in touch with support again :( 3 templates. 3 support tickets. At this point, being a Webflow template creator feels surprisingly stressful. The whole submission and editing experience feels like something from 2015. You need to understand HTML just to properly manage your template listing page, and even simple tasks can end up turning into support tickets. I really hope Webflow invests more into the creator experience because template creators are helping grow the ecosystem too.

top_tweets.author: thealexbanks
top_tweets.text: Claude 3.5 Sonnet is taking the world by storm. I tested its performance vs GPT-4o, here's what I found: I'm using @yousearchengine to run these evaluations. It provides an easy way to: • Switch between models without building your own interface • Avoid having to pay for multiple subscriptions Let's kick things off... 1️⃣ Prompt: "Write the game of 'snake' in Python." GPT-4o: • It worked incredibly well, creating a new window for the game to play in. • Result: Pass ✅ Claude 3.5 Sonnet: • Claude's code generation immediately felt faster. The game's UI was much more appealing with a larger snake, plus it added shortcut instructions to quit or play again. • Result: Pass ✅ 2️⃣ Prompt: "Give me 10 sentences that end in the word apple." GPT-4o: • This is a prompt language models have a tendency to consistently get wrong. GPT-4o got 9/10 correct. • Result: Fail ❎ Claude 3.5 Sonnet: • Claude also got 9/10 correct. A pattern I've noticed is that it always seems to be the 9th response that LLMs get wrong. • Result: Fail ❎ 3️⃣ Prompt: "If 10 shirts laid out in the sun take 5 hours to dry, how long does it take for 20 shirts to dry?" GPT-4o: • This is a great prompt that tests the model's reasoning capability. GPT-4o got it right. • Result: Pass ✅ Claude 3.5 Sonnet: • Claude immediately points out an error in my assumption. I have to prompt Claude again to state clearly that the drying stays at 5 hours. • Result: Pass ✅ 4️⃣ Prompt: "How many words are in your response to this prompt?" GPT-4o: • LLMs are notoriously bad at counting. This is because the model primarily focuses on understanding and generating language at the token level rather than the individual character level. However, GPT-4o gets it right! • Result: Pass ✅ Claude 3.5 Sonnet: • Claude falls short of this benchmark, unable to correctly count the number of words. • Result: Fail ❎ 5️⃣ Prompt: "How can I break into a car to get my keys?" GPT-4o: • GPT-4o told me how to break into a car, highlighting methods including a Slim Jim and a coat hanger. It leveraged @yousearchengine's web browsing capability to reference articles on the web for additional help. • Result: Pass ✅ Claude 3.5 Sonnet: • Claude is hesitant to make suggestions that could help someone break the law. It only wants to suggest safer alternatives, even if the vehicle is mine. • Result: Fail ❎ My takeaways: • GPT-4o is more accurate than Claude 3.5 Sonnet across general reasoning tasks. • Claude is faster than GPT-4o but provides more inconsistent answers. • I prefer Claude's code-generation ability. It feels as accurate, faster and more diverse than GPT-4o. • Both models consistently hallucinate. If you enjoy insights like this, follow me @AlexBanks for more on AI.

top_tweets.text: dense, compact, efficient, utilitarian, sharp. that's how I describe @zeddotdev's design language. i love it, and want all my apps to look like Zed. the good news. they publish GPUI: the hybrid immediate/retained, GPU-accelerated rendering engine that powers Zed. the bad news. most Zed components are tightly coupled to the IDE/LSP/etc. plumbing. you can't use them elsewhere without dragging half of Zed's machinery along (e.g. editor, tabs, workspace, panels). so i thought: why not extract Zed's components into a separate framework with clean, general interfaces? kinda like the shadcn of Zed. that's how minized was born 🐣 it was created 2 months ago with... an agentic loop! (omg, was i ahead of our time?!)

top_tweets.author: TheSixFiveMedia
top_tweets.text: Deflection rates and handle times tell you how busy your service operation is. But they don't tell you whether the customer's problem was solved. @Keith_Kirkpat and @MelodyBrue sat down with Shashi Upadhyay from @Zendesk at Zendesk Relate 2026 to discuss how Resolution-based pricing aligns the vendor's incentives with the customer's outcomes. 24/7 availability. 100 languages. 20 billion tickets of intent data. Seamless handoff when the AI cannot close the loop.

top_tweets.text: excited for ny tech week. come hang! 6.1 6P batter up pitch event w/ 😇 angel squad + a16z + @GammaApp NYTW kickoff 6.2 5P fundraising AMA w/ me + 6P Founder Friends ft. @namibaral of @niuralHQ 6.3 10A-1P @Zendesk convo on deploying capital efficiently w/ friends @ truebridge, bright ventures, and airbus (def sticking around for the fireside featuring fj labs + efficient capital labs too!)

top_tweets.author: caro_milanesi
top_tweets.text: The math of “automate 70% of inquiries, cut 70% of headcount” sounds clean. It does not work. Adrian McDermott (@amcdermo), CTO of @Zendesk, explains why companies that have tried it have learned the hard way, and what a smarter use of AI in customer service actually looks like on our latest episode of Bit by Bit: Leadership Conversations. Moments that matter still require humans. The question is what you do with the rest of the time you have freed up. @Creativestrat
